title: Samba

infobox.value: [Afro-Brazilian](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian") [batucada](/wiki/Batucada "Batucada") and rural traditional rhythms dances, especially *samba de roda*
Late 19th century in [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia"), and early 20th century, [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") and [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia"), Brazil

(Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˈsɐ̃bɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-Samba.ogg "File:Br-Samba.ogg")) is a broad term for many of the rhythms that compose the better known Brazilian music genres that originated in the [Afro-Brazilian](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian") communities of [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia") in the late 19th century and early 20th century,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZamith1995-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3) It is a name or prefix used for several rhythmic variants, such as **samba urbano carioca** (*urban Carioca samba*),[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015182-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFenerick200286-5) **samba de roda** (sometimes also called *rural samba*),[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandroni2010373-6) among many other forms of samba, mostly originated in the [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") and Bahia states. Having its roots in West African musical traditions,[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan201410,_15-7) especially those linked to the primitive rural samba[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2) of the [colonial](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil") and [imperial](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") periods,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015253-8) is considered one of the most important cultural phenomena in Brazil[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas20159-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan20149–10-10) and one of the country symbols.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019130-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201517–18,_43-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos2003109-13) Present in the [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") at least since the 19th century, the word "samba" was originally used to designate a "popular dance".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14) Over time, its meaning has been extended to a "[batuque](/w/index.php?title=Batuque_(Brazilian)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Batuque (Brazilian) (page does not exist)")-like circle dance", a [dance style](/w/index.php?title=Samba_(Braziian_dance)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samba (Braziian dance) (page does not exist)"), and also to a "music genre".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrugiuele2015105-15) This process of establishing itself as a musical genre began in the 1910s[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16) and it had its inaugural landmark in the song "[Pelo Telefone](/wiki/Pelo_Telefone "Pelo Telefone")", launched in 1917.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015219,_254-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201744-18) Despite being identified by its creators, the public, and the Brazilian music industry as "samba", this pioneering style was much more connected from the rhythmic and instrumental point of view to [maxixe](/wiki/Maxixe_(dance) "Maxixe (dance)") than to samba itself.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013126-20)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2015126-21)

**4** [time signature](/wiki/Time_signature "Time signature") varied[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14) with the conscious use of a sung chorus to a [batucada](/wiki/Batucada "Batucada") rhythm, with various stanzas of declaratory verses.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMello2000215-46) Its traditional instrumentation is composed of percussion instruments such as the [pandeiro](/wiki/Pandeiro "Pandeiro"), [cuíca](/wiki/Cu%C3%ADca "Cuíca"), [tamborim](/wiki/Tamborim "Tamborim"), [ganzá](/wiki/Ganz%C3%A1 "Ganzá") and [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo")[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015220-47)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBolão200922–44-48)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos2018107–109-49) accompaniment – whose inspiration is [choro](/wiki/Choro "Choro") – such as classical guitar and [cavaquinho](/wiki/Cavaquinho "Cavaquinho").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1990296–297-50)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2006153–154-51) In 2005 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") declared Samba de Roda part of [Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_of_Humanity "Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity"),[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamsonSandroni2013352-52) and in 2007, the Brazilian [National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Historic_and_Artistic_Heritage "National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage") declared Carioca samba and three of its matrices – samba de terreiro, partido-alto and samba

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Brazilian musical genre

This article is about the Brazilian music genre. For the Rio dance, see [Samba (Brazilian dance)](/wiki/Samba_(Brazilian_dance) "Samba (Brazilian dance)"). For the ballroom dance, see [Samba (ballroom dance)](/wiki/Samba_(ballroom_dance) "Samba (ballroom dance)"). For other uses, see [Samba (disambiguation)](/wiki/Samba_(disambiguation) "Samba (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(disambiguation) "Sambo (disambiguation)") or [Zamba](/wiki/Zamba_(artform) "Zamba (artform)").

**Samba** (Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˈsɐ̃bɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-Samba.ogg "File:Br-Samba.ogg")) is a broad term for many of the rhythms that compose the better known Brazilian music genres that originated in the [Afro-Brazilian](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian") communities of [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia") in the late 19th century and early 20th century,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZamith1995-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3) It is a name or prefix used for several rhythmic variants, such as **samba urbano carioca** (*urban Carioca samba*),[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015182-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFenerick200286-5) **samba de roda** (sometimes also called *rural samba*),[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandroni2010373-6) among many other forms of samba, mostly originated in the [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") and Bahia states. Having its roots in West African musical traditions,[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan201410,_15-7) especially those linked to the primitive rural samba[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2) of the [colonial](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil") and [imperial](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") periods,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015253-8) is considered one of the most important cultural phenomena in Brazil[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas20159-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan20149–10-10) and one of the country symbols.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019130-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201517–18,_43-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos2003109-13) Present in the [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") at least since the 19th century, the word "samba" was originally used to designate a "popular dance".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14) Over time, its meaning has been extended to a "[batuque](/w/index.php?title=Batuque_(Brazilian)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Batuque (Brazilian) (page does not exist)")-like circle dance", a [dance style](/w/index.php?title=Samba_(Braziian_dance)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samba (Braziian dance) (page does not exist)"), and also to a "music genre".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrugiuele2015105-15) This process of establishing itself as a musical genre began in the 1910s[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16) and it had its inaugural landmark in the song "[Pelo Telefone](/wiki/Pelo_Telefone "Pelo Telefone")", launched in 1917.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015219,_254-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201744-18) Despite being identified by its creators, the public, and the Brazilian music industry as "samba", this pioneering style was much more connected from the rhythmic and instrumental point of view to [maxixe](/wiki/Maxixe_(dance) "Maxixe (dance)") than to samba itself.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013126-20)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2015126-21)

Samba was modernly structured as a musical genre only in the late 1920s[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandroni200180-22) from the neighborhood of [Estácio](/wiki/Est%C3%A1cio,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Estácio, Rio de Janeiro") and soon extended to [Oswaldo Cruz](/wiki/Oswaldo_Cruz,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Oswaldo Cruz, Rio de Janeiro") and other parts of Rio through its [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015138,_182-23) Today synonymous with the rhythm of samba,[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201747-24) this new samba brought innovations in rhythm, melody and also in thematic aspects.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200939-25) Its rhythmic change based on a new percussive instrumental pattern resulted in a more drummed and syncopated style[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015276-26) – as opposed to the inaugural "samba–maxixe"[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200936-27) – notably characterized by a faster tempo, longer notes and a characterized cadence far beyond the simple ones used till then.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranceschi201052–53-28)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977708–709-29) Also the "Estácio paradigm" innovated in the formatting of samba as a song, with its musical organization in first and second parts in both melody and lyrics.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013126-20)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos200385-30)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200987-31) In this way, the sambistas of Estácio created, structured and redefined the urban Carioca samba as a genre in a modern and finished way.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013126-20) In this process of establishment as an urban and modern musical expression, the Carioca samba had the decisive role of samba schools, responsible for defining and legitimizing definitively the aesthetic bases of rhythm,[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200938-32) and radio broadcasting, which greatly contributed to the diffusion and popularization of the genre and its song singers.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015235-33) Thus, samba has achieved major projection throughout Brazil and has become one of the main [symbols](/wiki/National_symbol "National symbol") of Brazilian [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity").[[nb 1]](#cite_note-35)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-37)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockler20116-38)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan201410,_15-7) Once criminalized and rejected for its Brazilian origins, and definitely working-class music in its mythic origins, the genre has also received support from members of the upper classes and the country's cultural elite.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201543-39)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015183-40)

At the same time that it established itself as the genesis of samba,[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19) the "Estácio paradigm" paved the way for its fragmentation into new sub-genres and styles of composition and interpretation throughout the 20th century.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015279-41) Mainly from the so-called "golden age" of Brazilian music,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano1997241-42) samba received abundant categorizations, some of which denote solid and well-accepted derivative strands, such as [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova"), [pagode](/wiki/Pagode "Pagode"), [partido alto](/wiki/Partido_alto "Partido alto"), samba de breque, [samba-canção](/wiki/Samba-can%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Samba-canção"), [samba de enredo](/wiki/Samba-enredo "Samba-enredo") and samba de terreiro, while other nomenclatures were somewhat more imprecise, such as samba do barulho (literally "noise samba"), samba epistolar ("epistolary samba") ou samba fonético ("phonetic samba")[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013127-43) – and some merely derogatory – such as sambalada,[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015269-44) sambolero or sambão joia.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015271-45)

The modern samba that emerged at the beginning of the 20th century is predominantly in a **2**  
**4** [time signature](/wiki/Time_signature "Time signature") varied[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14) with the conscious use of a sung chorus to a [batucada](/wiki/Batucada "Batucada") rhythm, with various stanzas of declaratory verses.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMello2000215-46) Its traditional instrumentation is composed of percussion instruments such as the [pandeiro](/wiki/Pandeiro "Pandeiro"), [cuíca](/wiki/Cu%C3%ADca "Cuíca"), [tamborim](/wiki/Tamborim "Tamborim"), [ganzá](/wiki/Ganz%C3%A1 "Ganzá") and [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo")[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015220-47)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBolão200922–44-48)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos2018107–109-49) accompaniment – whose inspiration is [choro](/wiki/Choro "Choro") – such as classical guitar and [cavaquinho](/wiki/Cavaquinho "Cavaquinho").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1990296–297-50)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2006153–154-51) In 2005 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") declared Samba de Roda part of [Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_of_Humanity "Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity"),[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamsonSandroni2013352-52) and in 2007, the Brazilian [National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Historic_and_Artistic_Heritage "National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage") declared Carioca samba and three of its matrices – samba de terreiro, partido-alto and samba de enredo – as [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage") in Brazil.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan201423-53)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPortal_Iphan2007-54)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUchôa2007-55)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFigueiredo2007-56)

## Etymology and definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and definition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Batuque.jpg/250px-Batuque.jpg)](/wiki/File:Batuque.jpg)

"Batuque" (1835), a painting by [Johann Moritz Rugendas](/wiki/Johann_Moritz_Rugendas "Johann Moritz Rugendas")[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEItaú_Cultural2020-57)

There is no consensus among experts on the [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") of the term "samba". A traditionalist view that defends that the etymon comes from the [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") was in the *Diário de Pernambuco* in 1830.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiário_de_Pernambuco18302098–2099-58) The term was documented in the publication in a note opposing the sending of soldiers to the *c*ountryside of [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco") State as a disciplinary measure, as there they could be idle and entertained with "fishing of corrals [traps to catch fish], and climbing coconut trees, in whose hobbies viola and samba will be welcomed ".[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiário_de_Pernambuco18302098–2099-58) Another old appearance was recorded in the humorous [Recife](/wiki/Recife "Recife") newspaper *O Carapuceiro*, dated February 1838,[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes_da_Gama18381-59) when Father Miguel Lopes Gama of Sacramento wrote against what he called "the samba d'almocreve" – not referring to the future musical genre, but a kind of merriment (dance drama) popular for black people of that time. According to Hiram Araújo da Costa, over the centuries, the festival of dances of enslaved people in Bahia were called samba.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlein2007-60) In [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), the word only became known at the end of the 19th century, when it was linked to rural festivities, to the area of Black people and to the "north" of the country, that is, the [Brazilian Northeast](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil").[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeto2018-61)

For many years of the Brazilian [colonial](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil") and [imperial](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") history, the terms "batuque" or "[samba](/wiki/Samba_(Brazilian_dance) "Samba (Brazilian dance)")" were used in any manifestation of [African origins](/wiki/African_Origins "African Origins") that brought together dances (mainly [umbigada](/wiki/Umbigada "Umbigada")), songs and uses of [Black people](/wiki/Afro-Brazilians "Afro-Brazilians") instruments.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14) At the end of the 19th century, "samba" was present in the [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), designating different types of popular dances performed by [African slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_Brazil "Slavery in Brazil") (xiba, [fandango](/wiki/Fandango "Fandango"), catereté, candomblé, [baião](/wiki/Bai%C3%A3o_(music) "Baião (music)")) that assumed its own characteristics in each [Brazilian state](/wiki/Federative_units_of_Brazil "Federative units of Brazil"), not only by the diversity of the ethnic groups of the [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora_in_the_Americas "African diaspora in the Americas"), but also the peculiarity of each region in which they were settlers.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015247-14) In the twentieth century, the term was gaining new meanings, as for a "circle dance similar to batuque" and a "genre of popular song".[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrugiuele2015105-15)

The use of the word in a musical context was documented as early as 1913 in the song *Em casa de baiana*, registered as a "samba de partido-alto";[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoura198377-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva2016-63) then in the following year, for the works *A viola está magoada*[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva2016-63)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVasconcelos197725-64) and *Moleque vagabundo;*[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMS2019c-65)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano199741-66) finally, in 1916, for the famous song *[Pelo Telefone](/wiki/Pelo_Telefone "Pelo Telefone")*, released as "samba carnavalesco" ("carnival samba")[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMS2019a-67)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMS2019b-68) and regarded as the founding landmark of modern Carioca samba.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_Neto201790-69)

## Roots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Roots")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/22/Hil%C3%A1rio_Jovino_Ferreira.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hil%C3%A1rio_Jovino_Ferreira.jpg)

[Hilário Jovino Ferreira](/wiki/Hil%C3%A1rio_Jovino_Ferreira "Hilário Jovino Ferreira") founded the first carnival rancho in Rio.

### Rural tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Rural tradition")]

During a folkloric research mission in the [Northeast Region](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil") of 1938, the writer [Mário de Andrade](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_de_Andrade "Mário de Andrade") noticed that, in rural areas, the term "samba" was associated with the event where the dance was performed, the way of dancing the samba and the music performed for the dance.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarneiro2005329-70) Urban Carioca samba was influenced by several traditions associated with the universe of rural communities throughout Brazil.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015253-8) The folklorist Oneida Alvarenga was the first expert to list primitive popular dances of the type: *coco*, *tambor de crioula*, *[lundu](/wiki/Lundu_(dance) "Lundu (dance)")*, *[chula](/wiki/Chula_(music) "Chula (music)")* or *fandango*, *baiano*, *cateretê*, *quimbere*, *mbeque*, *caxambu* and *xiba*.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlvarenga1960130–171-71) To this list, Jorge Sabino and Raul Lody added *samba de coco*, *sambada* (also called *coco de roda*), *samba de matuto*, *samba de caboclo*, and *[jongo](/wiki/Jongo "Jongo")*.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESabinoLody201154-72)

One of the most important forms of dance in the constitution of Carioca samba choreography,[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015263-73) the *[samba de roda](/wiki/Samba_(Brazilian_dance)#Samba_de_roda "Samba (Brazilian dance)")*, '[circle samba](/wiki/Circle_dance "Circle dance")', practiced in Bahia's [Recôncavo](/wiki/Rec%C3%B4ncavo_Baiano "Recôncavo Baiano"), was typically danced outdoors by a [soloist](/wiki/Soloist_(music) "Soloist (music)"), while other participants of the circle would take charge of the singing – alternating between solo and chorus parts[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3) – and of accompanying instrumental performance.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015263-73) The three basic dance-steps of Bahian circle samba were named *corta-a-jaca*, *separa-o-visgo*, and *apanha-o-bago* (literally and respectively, "cut-the-jackfruit", "separate-the-birdlime", and "pick-the-grape"), in addition to one intended to be danced by women only.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3) In their research on Bahian samba, Roberto Mendes and Waldomiro Junior examined that some elements from other cultures, such as the [Arab pandeiro](/wiki/Pandeiro "Pandeiro") and the Portuguese viola, were gradually incorporated into the singing and rhythm of African [batuques](/wiki/Batuque_(Brazil) "Batuque (Brazil)"), whose best-known variants were *samba corrido* and *samba chulado*.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMendesJunior200854-74)

In the [São Paulo state](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), another primitive modality of known rural samba developed, practiced basically in cities along the [Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River") – from [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") city to the river's middle course[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015250-75) – and traditionally divided into *samba de bumbo*, characterized by instrumental percussion with a bass drum,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015250-75) and *[batuque](/wiki/Batuque_(Brazil) "Batuque (Brazil)") de [umbigada](/wiki/Umbigada "Umbigada")*, with [tambu](/wiki/Tambu_(music)#Instrumentation "Tambu (music)"), quinjengue and guaiá for the instrumentation.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampolim20099-76)

Essentially made up of two parts (choir and solo) usually performed on the fly, the [partido alto](/wiki/Partido_alto "Partido alto") was – and still is – the most traditional sung variant of rural samba in the state of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015252-77) Originating in [Greater Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Greater_Rio_de_Janeiro "Greater Rio de Janeiro"), it is the combination, according to Lopes and Simas, of Bahian circle-samba, *calango* singing, and a kind of transition from rural samba to what would come to be urban 20th century Rio samba.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015252-77)

### Criminalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Criminalization")]

In its beginnings, samba was heavily criminalized by the Brazilian government. Born in the [favelas](/wiki/Favela "Favela"), it was a distinctly Afro Brazilian musical genre that brought people together in community and celebration, which was not well-seen or -received by the Brazilian elite, who deemed it tasteless, immoral and inferior. Such attitude was grounded in [racism](/wiki/Racism_in_Brazil "Racism in Brazil") and [classism](/wiki/Class_discrimination "Class discrimination"), besides [religious intolerance](/wiki/Religious_intolerance "Religious intolerance"): samba's incorporation of [African drumming](/wiki/African_drumming "African drumming") was commonly associated with [Afro Brazilian religions](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian_religion "Afro-Brazilian religion"), which have long been demonized and discriminated against in Brazil, especially so in the early 20th century, when samba was gaining traction.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiranda2021-78)

Many early composers were thought to be leaders of cults of African origin, therefore samba faced policed persecution. Any samba gathering would be swiftly shut down, with musicians arrested and their instruments destroyed. As a result, samba had to go underground, relying on community members to assume the risk of persecution to have samba parties out of their homes. Ultimately, the genre became a hallmark of Brazilian culture and a highlight of [Carnival](/wiki/Brazilian_Carnival "Brazilian Carnival"), but it was not ever thus, as in its origins practicing samba was defiance against the government.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalinsky1996-79)

### Roots of the Carioca Carnival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Roots of the Carioca Carnival")]

During colonial Brazil, many public [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") events used to attract all social segments, including Black and enslaved people, who took the celebrations as opportunities to express themselves authentically, in their original, native way, with such cultural manifestations as the crowning revelry of the Congo Kings, and the Bantu revelry (called [cucumbi](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cucumbi "pt:Cucumbi")) in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbreu1994184–185-80) Gradually were those once exclusively Black celebrations being disconnected from the Catholic roots and rites of Carnival, eventually morphing into the [Brazilian Carnival](/wiki/Brazilian_Carnival "Brazilian Carnival").[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019110-81) From the cucumbis emerged the [samba school](/wiki/Samba_school "Samba school") called *cordões cariocas* (lit. 'Carioca strings'), which celebrated Brazilianness, or the mixed heritage of the Brazilian people, e.g. by presenting Black people in indigenous dress.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019110-81) At the end of the 19th century, on the initiative of composer and samba pioneer [Hilário Jovino](/wiki/Hil%C3%A1rio_Jovino_Ferreira "Hilário Jovino Ferreira"), the samba ranch *ranchos de reis*, 'ranches of kings' (later known as *ranchos de carnaval*, 'Carnival ranches'), was created in the state of [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019110–111-82) One of the most important ranches in Rio's Carnival was Ameno Resedá.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019111-83) Created in 1907, the self-titled *racho-escola* ("ranch-school") became a model for Carnival performances-in-procession and for future samba schools to be founded in the hills and outskirts of Rio de Janeiro.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019111-83)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The urban Carioca samba")]

### Beginnings in a Bahian [terreiro](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Templos_afro-brasileiros#Terreiro "pt:Templos afro-brasileiros")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Beginnings in a Bahian terreiro")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Ernesto_Santos_%28Donga%29_2_%281972%29.jpg/250px-Ernesto_Santos_%28Donga%29_2_%281972%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ernesto_Santos_(Donga)_2_(1972).jpg)

Donga recorded "[Pelo Telefone](/wiki/Pelo_Telefone "Pelo Telefone")", considered the founding landmark of samba.

A political and sociocultural epicenter of Brazil, based on [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_Brazil "Slavery in Brazil"), Rio de Janeiro was strongly influenced by [African culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Africa "Culture of Africa").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989208-84) In the middle of the 19th century, more than half the population of the city – then capital of the [Brazilian Empire](/wiki/Brazilian_Empire "Brazilian Empire") – was formed by [black slaves](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989208-84) In the early 1890s, Rio had more than half a million inhabitants, of whom only half were born in the city, while the other part came from the old Brazilian imperial provinces, mainly from [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201527-85) In search of better living conditions, this influx of black Bahians to Rio lands increased considerably after [the abolition of slavery in Brazil](/wiki/Lei_%C3%81urea "Lei Áurea").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989209-86) Called "Pequena Africa" ("Little Africa") by [Heitor dos Prazeres](/wiki/Heitor_dos_Prazeres "Heitor dos Prazeres"), this Afro-Bahian diaspora community in the country's capital settled in the vicinity of the Rio de Janeiro port area and, after the urban reforms of Mayor [Pereira Passos](/wiki/Francisco_Pereira_Passos "Francisco Pereira Passos"), in the neighborhoods of [Saúde](/wiki/Sa%C3%BAde,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Saúde, Rio de Janeiro") and [Cidade Nova](/wiki/Cidade_Nova,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Cidade Nova, Rio de Janeiro").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989209-86) Through the action of black Bahians living in Rio, new habits, customs and values of Afro-Bahian matrixes were introduced that influenced the culture of Rio,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989209-86)[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019113-87) especially in popular events such as the traditional Festa da Penha and [Carnival](/wiki/Rio_Carnival "Rio Carnival").[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019113-87) Black women from Salvador and Bahia's Recôncavo,[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMendes2016-88) the "Tias Baianas" (literally, "Bahian Aunts") founded the first [Candomblé](/wiki/Candombl%C3%A9 "Candomblé") terreiros,[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201527-85) introduced the [cowrie-shell divination](/wiki/Cowrie-shell_divination "Cowrie-shell divination")[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989216-89) and disseminated the mysteries of the African-based religions of the Jeje-Nagô tradition in the city.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENunes_Neto201946-90) In addition to candomblé, the residences or terreiros of the aunts of Bahia hosted various community activities, such as cooking and the pagodes, where urban Rio samba would develop.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989215-91)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlencar198179-92)

Among the most well-known Bahian aunts in Rio, were the Tias Sadata, Bibiana, Fê, Rosa Olé, Amélia do Aragão, Veridiana, Mônica, Perciliana de Santo Amaro and [Ciata](/wiki/Tia_Ciata "Tia Ciata").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989216-89)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015290-93) A place for meetings around religion, cuisine, dance and music,[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989215-91) [Tia Ciata](/wiki/Tia_Ciata "Tia Ciata")'s home was frequented both by samba musicians and [pais-de-santo](/wiki/Pai-de-santo "Pai-de-santo") as well as by influential intellectuals and politicians from Rio de Janeiro society.[[nb 3]](#cite_note-95)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlmirante197747-96) Among some of its members regulars were [Sinhô](/w/index.php?title=Sinh%C3%B4&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sinhô (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinh%C3%B4 "pt:Sinhô")], [Pixinguinha](/wiki/Pixinguinha "Pixinguinha"), Heitor dos Prazeres, [João da Baiana](/w/index.php?title=Jo%C3%A3o_da_Baiana&action=edit&redlink=1 "João da Baiana (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_da_Baiana "pt:João da Baiana")], [Donga](/wiki/Donga_(musician) "Donga (musician)") and [Caninha](/w/index.php?title=Caninha_(composer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caninha (composer) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caninha "pt:Caninha")], as well as some journalists and intellectuals, such as [João do Rio](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_do_Rio "João do Rio"), [Manuel Bandeira](/wiki/Manuel_Bandeira "Manuel Bandeira"), [Mário de Andrade](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_de_Andrade "Mário de Andrade") and [Francisco Guimarães](/w/index.php?title=Francisco_Guimar%C3%A3es&action=edit&redlink=1 "Francisco Guimarães (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francisco_Guimar%C3%A3es "pt:Francisco Guimarães")] (popularly known as Vagalume).[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989216-89) It was in this environment that Vagalume, then a columnist for [Jornal do Brazil](/w/index.php?title=Jornal_do_Brazil&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jornal do Brazil (page does not exist)"), witnessed the birth of "O Macaco É Outro" in October 1916.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989222-97) According to the journalist, this samba immediately won the support of the popular people who left singing the music in an animated block.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1989222-97) Donga registered the work in [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") and, on 27 November of that year, declared himself as its author in the National Library, where it was registered as "carnival samba" called "[Pelo Telefone](/wiki/Pelo_Telefone "Pelo Telefone")".[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977236-98)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015219-99) Shortly after, the score was used in three recordings at Casa Edison record label.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201599-100) One of them interpreted by [Baiano](/w/index.php?title=Baiano_(singer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baiano (singer) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baiano_(cantor) "pt:Baiano (cantor)")][[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMS2019a-67)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes197766-101) with the accompaniment of classical guitar, cavaquinho and clarinet.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiron2016-102) Released in [78 rpm](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") format on 19 January 1917, "Pelo Telefone" became a huge hit in that year's Rio carnival.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015219,_254-17)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiron2016-102) Two instrumental versions were also released – recorded by Banda Odeon and Banda de 1º Battalion of the Police of Bahia – in 1917 and 1918 respectively.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMS2019b-68)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMMuB2020a-103)[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMMuB2020b-104)

The success of "Pelo Telefone" marked the official beginning of samba as a song genre.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977236-98)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiron2016-102) Its primacy as "the first samba in history" has, however, been questioned by some scholars, on the grounds that the work was only the first samba under this categorization to be successful.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoura198377-62)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_Neto201790-69)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuniz197627-105)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano199753-106) Before, "Em casa da baiana" was recorded by Alfredo Carlos Bricio, declared to the National Library as "samba de partido-alto" in 1913,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoura198377-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva2016-63) "A viola está magoada", by [Catulo da Paixão Cearense](/wiki/Catulo_da_Paix%C3%A3o_Cearense "Catulo da Paixão Cearense"), released as "samba" by Baiano and Júlia the following year,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoura198377-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva2016-63)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuniz197627-105) and "Moleque vagabundo", "samba" by Lourival de Carvalho, also in 1914.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015254-2)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMS2019c-65)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano199741-66)

Another debate related to "Pelo Telefone" concerns Donga's exclusive authorship, which was soon contested by some of his contemporaries who accused him of appropriating a collective, anonymous creation, registering it as his own.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoraes2016-107)[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArbix2008-108) The central part of the song would have been conceived in the traditional improvisations in meetings at [Tia Ciata](/wiki/Tia_Ciata "Tia Ciata")'s house.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoraes2016-107) Sinhô claimed the authorship of the chorus "ai, se rolinha, sinhô, sinhô"[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiron2016-102) and created another song lyrics in response to Donga.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201583-109) However, Sinhô himself, who would consolidate himself in the 1920s as the first important figure of samba,[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVagalume197831–33-110) was accused of appropriating other people's songs or verses – to which he justified himself with the famous maxim that samba was "like a bird" in the air, it is "whoever gets it first".[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977720-111)[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonteiro201066-112) This defense is part of a period in which the figure of the popular composer was not that of the individual who composed or organized sounds, but the one who registered and disseminated the songs.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200981-113) In the era of [mechanical recordings](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record"), musical compositions – under the pretext of ensuring that there was no [plagiarism](/wiki/Plagiarism "Plagiarism") – did not belong to composers, but to publishers[[nb 4]](#cite_note-115) and, later, to record labels,[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200982-116) a reality modified only with the advent of electrical recordings, when the right to intellectual property of the work became individual and inalienable to the composer.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200982-116) In any case, it was because "Pelo Telefone" that samba gained notoriety as a product in the Brazilian music industry.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiron2016-102)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015148-117) Gradually, the nascent urban samba was gaining popularity in Rio de Janeiro, especially at the Festa da Penha and Carnival.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019113-87) In October, the Festa da Penha became a great event for composers from Cidade Nova who wanted to publicize their compositions in the expectation that they would be released at the following carnival.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200978-118) Another promoter during this period was the [Revue](/wiki/Revue "Revue") shows, a place that enshrined [Aracy Cortes](/wiki/Aracy_Cortes "Aracy Cortes") as one of the first successful singers of the new popular song genre.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977205-119)

The solidification of the electric recording system made it possible for the recording industry to launch new sambas by singers with less powerful voices,[[nb 5]](#cite_note-121) such as [Carmen Miranda](/wiki/Carmen_Miranda "Carmen Miranda")[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201553-122) and [Mário Reis](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Reis_(singer) "Mário Reis (singer)"), performers who became references when creating a new way of interpreting the most natural and spontaneous samba, without so many ornaments, as opposed to the tradition of [belcanto style](/wiki/Bel_canto "Bel canto").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977649-123)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996a18–19-124)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201740-125) These recordings followed an aesthetic pattern characterized by structural similarities to the [lundu](/wiki/Lundu_(dance) "Lundu (dance)") and, mainly, to the [maxixe](/wiki/Maxixe_(dance) "Maxixe (dance)").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19) Because of this, this type of samba is considered by scholars as "samba-maxixe" or "samba amaxixado".[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200931-126) Although the samba practiced in the festivities of Bahian communities in Rio was an urban stylization of the ancestral "samba de roda" in Bahia,[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfegê200724-127) characterized by a high party samba with refrains sung to the marked rhythm of the palms and the plates shaved with knives, this samba it was also influenced by the maxixe.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200931–32-128) It was in the following decade that a new model of samba would be born, from the hills of Rio de Janeiro, quite distinct from that of the amaxixado style associated with the communities of Cidade Nova.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019112-16)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19)

### Samba do Estácio, the genesis of urban samba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Samba do Estácio, the genesis of urban samba")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Na Pavuna"](/wiki/File:Na_Pavuna-Almirante-Homero_Dornelas1929.ogg "File:Na Pavuna-Almirante-Homero Dornelas1929.ogg")

Composed by Homero Dornelas, recorded by Bando de Tangarás; hit in the Rio carnival of 1930

---

["O que será de mim"](/wiki/File:O_QUE_SER%C3%81_DE_MIM-_Francisco_Alves_e_M%C3%A1rio_Reis_1931.ogg "File:O QUE SERÁ DE MIM- Francisco Alves e Mário Reis 1931.ogg")

Composed by Ismael Silva, Nilson Bastos and Francisco Alves; recorded by Francisco Alves and Mário Reis in 1931

---

["Agora é Cinza"](/wiki/File:Agora_%C3%A9_Cinza-Mario_Reis-Bide_e_Mar%C3%A7al1933.ogg "File:Agora é Cinza-Mario Reis-Bide e Marçal1933.ogg")

Composed by Bide and Marçal, recorded by Mario Reis in 1933

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Samba_dance_pattern.png/330px-Samba_dance_pattern.png)](/wiki/File:Samba_dance_pattern.png)

Samba rhythm[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlatter2007-129)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Ismael_Silva.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Ismael_Silva.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ismael_Silva.tif)

The sambista Ismael Silva was one of the great composers of Estácio's samba that emerged in the 1920s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Heitor_dos_Prazeres%2C_1961.jpg/250px-Heitor_dos_Prazeres%2C_1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heitor_dos_Prazeres,_1961.jpg)

The sambista Heitor dos Prazeres participated in the foundation of the first Brazilian samba schools.

Between the end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century, in the context of the [First Brazilian Republic](/wiki/First_Brazilian_Republic "First Brazilian Republic"), the poor strata of Rio de Janeiro faced serious economic issues related to their survival in the federal capital, such as the imposition of new taxes resulting from the provision of [public services](/wiki/Public_service "Public service") (such as electric lighting, water and sewage, modern pavements), new legislation that imposed architectural norms and restrictions for urban buildings, and the prohibition on the exercise of certain professions or economic practices linked to subsistence, especially of the poorest.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenchimol1990287-130) The situation of this population worsened further with the urban reforms in the [center of Rio](/wiki/Centro,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Centro, Rio de Janeiro"), whose widening or opening of roads required the destruction of several tenements and popular housing in the region.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattos200853-131)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200924-132)

As a result, these homeless residents were temporarily occupying slopes in the vicinity of these old demolished buildings, such as [Morro da Providência](/wiki/Morro_da_Provid%C3%AAncia "Morro da Providência") (mainly occupied by former residents of the Cabeça de Porco tenement[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVaz198635-133) and former soldiers of the [War of Canudos](/wiki/War_of_Canudos "War of Canudos"))[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200925-134) and Morro de Santo Antonio (especially by ex-combatants of the [Brazilian Naval Revolts](/wiki/Revolta_da_Armada "Revolta da Armada")).[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbreuVaz1991489-135) In a short time, this type of temporary housing was permanently established in the urban landscape of Rio, originating the first favelas in the city.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattos200859-136) From the increase in the populations expelled from the tenements and the arrival of new poor migrants to the capital of the Republic, the favelas grew rapidly and spread through the hills settlements and suburban areas of Rio.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200925-134)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattos2008248-137)

It was in this scenario that a new type of samba would be born during the second half of the 1920s, called "samba do Estácio", which would constitute the genesis of urban Carioca samba[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201511-19) by creating a new pattern so revolutionary that its innovations last until the days current.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVianna199534-36)[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos200384–85-138) Located close to [Praça Onze](/wiki/Pra%C3%A7a_Onze_Station "Praça Onze Station") and housing Morro do São Carlos, the neighborhood of [Estácio](/wiki/Est%C3%A1cio,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Estácio, Rio de Janeiro") was a center of convergence of public transport, mainly of trams that served the North Zone of the city.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015123-139) Its proximity to the nascent hills settlements as well as its primacy in the formation of this new samba ended up linking its musical production, from [urban train lines](/wiki/SuperVia "SuperVia"), to the favelas and suburbs of Rio, such as [Morro da Mangueira](/wiki/Mangueira "Mangueira"),[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão199791–92-140)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200936-27) and the suburban neighborhood of [Osvaldo Cruz](/wiki/Oswaldo_Cruz,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Oswaldo Cruz, Rio de Janeiro").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015138,_182-23)

Estácio's samba was distinguished from Cidade Nova's samba both in thematic aspects, as well as in the melody and rhythm.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200939-25) Made for the parades of the carnival blocs in the neighborhood,[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015124-141) the samba do Estácio innovated with a faster tempo, longer notes and a cadence beyond the traditional palms.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranceschi201052–53-28)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977708–709-29) Another structural change resulting from this samba was the valorization of the "second part" of the compositions: instead of using the typical improvisation of the samba circles of the alto party or carnival parades, there was the consolidation of pre-established sequences, which would have a theme – for example, everyday problems[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranceschi201052–53-28) – and the possibility of fitting everything within the standards of [the phonograph recordings](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") of 78 rpm at the time[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos200385-30) – something like three minutes on 10-inch discs.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESadie1994385-142) In comparison to the works of the first generation of Donga, Sinhô and company, the sambas produced by the Estácio group also stood out for a greater [countermetricity](/wiki/Metre_(music) "Metre (music)"),[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200954-143) which can be evidenced in a testimony by [Ismael Silva](/wiki/Ismael_Silva_(musician) "Ismael Silva (musician)") about the innovations introduced by him and his companions in the new urban samba in Rio:

> At that time, samba did not work for carnival groups to walk on the street as we see today. I started noticing that there was this thing. The samba was like this: *tan tantan tan tantan*. It was not possible. How would a bloc get out on the street like that? Then, we started making a samba like this: *bum bum paticumbum pugurumdum*.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996a242-144)

— Ismael Silva

The intuitive [onomatopoeia](/wiki/Onomatopoeia "Onomatopoeia") built by Ismael Silva tried to explain the rhythmic change operated by the sambistas of Estácio with the *bum bum paticumbum pugurumdum* of the [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo") in marking the cadence of the samba, making it a more syncopated rhythm.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200955-145)[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201746–47-146) It was, therefore, a break with the samba *tan tantan tan tantan* irradiated from the Bahian aunts meetings.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200939-25)

Thus, at the end of the 1920s, the modern carioca samba had two distinct models: the primitive urban samba of Cidade Nova and the new syncopated samba of the Estácio group.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200933-147) However, while the Bahian community enjoyed a certain social legitimacy, including the protection of important personalities of Rio society who supported and frequented the musical circles of the "Pequena Africa",[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximoDidier1990138-148) the new Estaciano sambistas suffered socio-cultural discrimination, including through police repression.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200960-149) A popular neighborhood with a large Black/mixed contingent , Estácio was one of the great strongholds of poor samba musicians situated between marginality and social integration, who ended up being stigmatized by the upper classes in Rio as "dangerous" rascals.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão199791–92-140)[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015180-150) Because of this infamous brand, the Estaciano samba suffered great social prejudice in its origin.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximoDidier1990138-148)

To avoid police harassment and gain social legitimacy, Estácio's samba musicians decided to link their batucadas to carnival samba and organized themselves in what they christened as samba schools.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200939–41-151)[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201744–46-152)

> At the end of the carnival, samba has continued because we did samba all year. At Café Apolo, Café do Compadre, across the street, at the backyard feijoadas or at dawn, on street corners and in bars. Then the police used to come and bother us. But it didn't bother the guys of (carnival rancho) Amor, which had a headquarter and license to parade at the carnival. We decided to organize a carnival bloc, even without a license, that could allow us to go out at the carnival and do samba all year round. Organization and respect, without fights or huffing, were important. It was called "Deixa Falar" as it despises the middle class ladies of the neighborhood who used to call people a vagabond. We were malandros, in a good way, but vagabonds weren't.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1974230-153)

— Bide

According to Ismael Silva – also a founder of [Deixa Falar](/wiki/Deixa_Falar "Deixa Falar") and the creator of the expression "samba school" – the term was inspired by the Normal school that once existed in Estácio,[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015116-154) and therefore the samba schools would form "samba teachers".[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996a241-155) Although the primacy of the country's first samba school is contested by [Portela](/wiki/Portela_(samba_school) "Portela (samba school)") and [Mangueira](/wiki/Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Primeira_de_Mangueira "Estação Primeira de Mangueira"),[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019113-87)[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201750–51-156) Deixa Falar was a pioneer in spreading the term in its quest to establish a different organization from the [carnival blocks](/wiki/Carnival_block "Carnival block") of that time[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200942-157) and also the first carnival association to use the group in the future known as [bateria](/wiki/Bateria "Bateria"), a unit made up of percussion instruments such as the surdo, [tamborims](/wiki/Tamborim "Tamborim") and [cuícas](/wiki/Cu%C3%ADca "Cuíca"), which – when joining the already used [pandeiros](/wiki/Pandeiro "Pandeiro") and [shakers](/wiki/Shaker_(musical_instrument) "Shaker (musical instrument)") – gave a more "marching" characteristic to the samba of the parades.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos200384–85-138)[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201747–49-158)

In 1929, the sambista and [babalawo](/wiki/Babalawo "Babalawo") [Zé Espinguela](/wiki/Z%C3%A9_Espinguela "Zé Espinguela") organized the first contest among the first samba schools in Rio: Deixa Falar, Mangueira and Oswaldo Cruz (later Portela).[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015117-159)[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201750–51-156) The dispute did not involve parede[*[check spelling](/wiki/Template:Typo_help_inline "Template:Typo help inline")*], but a competition to choose the best samba theme among these carnival groups – whose winner is the samba "A Tristeza Me Persegue", by Heitor dos Prazeres, one of Oswaldo Cruz's representatives.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015117-159) Deixa Falar was disqualified for the use of a flute and tie by Benedito Lacerda, then representative of the Estácio group.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernandes200172-160) This veto on wind instruments became the rule from then on[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200946-161) – including for the first parade between them, organized in 1932 by journalist [Mario Filho](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Filho "Mário Filho") and sponsored by the daily Mundo Sportivo[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015117-159) -, because it differentiated schools from carnival ranchos with the appreciation of batucadas, which would definitely mark the aesthetic bases of samba from then on.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200946-161)

Estácio's batucado and syncopated samba represented an aesthetic break with Cidade Nova's maxixe-style samba.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200936-27) In turn, the first generation of samba did not accept the innovations created by the samba musicians of the hill, seen as a misrepresentation of the genre[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos200384-162) or even designated as "[march](/wiki/March_(music) "March (music)")".[[nb 6]](#cite_note-164) For musicians such as Donga and Sinhô, samba was synonymous with maxixe – a kind of the last Brazilian stage of [European polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka").[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilvaFilho199882-165) For the samba musicians from the hills of Rio, samba was the last Brazilian stage of Angolan drumming that they proposed to teach to Brazilian society through samba schools.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilvaFilho199882-165) This generational conflict, however, did not last for long, and Estácio's samba established itself as the rhythm par excellence of Rio's urban samba during the 1930s.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandroni200180-22)[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200960-149)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReijonen201747-24)

Between 1931 and 1940 samba was the most recorded genre music in Brazil, with almost 1/3 of the total repertoire – 2,176 sambas songs in a universe of 6,706 compositions.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano199767-166) Sambas and [marchinhas](/wiki/Marchinha "Marchinha") together made up the percentages just over half of the repertoire recorded in that period.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano199767-166) Thanks to the new electric recording technology, it was possible to capture the percussive instruments present in samba schools.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200985-120) The samba "Na Pavuna", performed by Bando de Tangarás, was the first recorded in studio with the percussion that would characterize the genre from there: tamborim, surdo, pandeiro, ganzá, cuíca, among others.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015150-167) Although there was the presence of these percussive instruments, the samba recordings in the studio were characterized by the predominance of musical arrangements of orchestrated tone with [brass](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument") and [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument").[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200986-168) This orchestral pattern was mainly printed by Jewish arrangers, among them Simon Bountman, Romeu Ghipsmanm, Isaac Kolman and Arnold Gluckman, conductors whose erudite formation ended up giving a European [symphonic sound](/wiki/Symphony "Symphony") in the counter-metric rhythm and batucada of the samba from Estacio.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranceschi2002292-169)

Another reason for the success of the new samba in the music industry was the introduction of the "second part", which stimulated the establishment of partnerships between the composers.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200987-31) For example, one composer created the chorus of a samba and another composer conceived the second part, as occurred in the partnership between Ismael Silva and [Noel Rosa](/wiki/Noel_Rosa "Noel Rosa") in "Para Me Livrar do Mal".[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximoDidier1990209-170) With the growing demand for new sambas by the singers, the practice of buying and selling compositions has also become common.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019114-171)[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015100-172) This transaction usually took place in two different ways: the author negotiated only the sale of the samba recording – that is, he remained as the author of the composition, but he would not receive any part of the gains obtained from the sales of the records, which were divided between the buyer and the record label[[nb 7]](#cite_note-173) – or the entire composition – that is, the real author completely lost the rights to his samba, including authorship.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry200870–71-174) In some cases, the sambista sold the partnership to the buyer and also received a portion of the profits from the sales of the records.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry200870–71-174) Selling a samba meant the composer had a chance to see his production publicized – especially when he did not yet enjoy the same prestige acquired by the first generation samba composers – and also a way to make up for his own financial difficulties.[[nb 8]](#cite_note-176)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry200870–71-174) For the buyer, it was the possibility to renew his repertoire, record more records and earn sales, and further consolidate his artistic career.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFenerick2002168-177) Artists with good contact with record labels, the popular singers [Francisco Alves](/wiki/Francisco_Alves_(singer) "Francisco Alves (singer)") and [Mário Reis](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Reis_(singer) "Mário Reis (singer)") were adepts of this practicea,[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977639,_649-178)[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVagalume197829-179) having acquired sambas from composers such as [Cartola](/wiki/Cartola "Cartola")[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximoDidier1990210-180)[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996a272-181)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECometti2004-182) and Ismael Silva.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximoDidier1990210-180)[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiron200198–100-183)[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996a244–245-184)

### Radio era and popularization of samba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Radio era and popularization of samba")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Uva de caminhão"](/wiki/File:UVA_DE_CAMINH%C3%83O_-_Carmen_Miranda_1939.ogg "File:UVA DE CAMINHÃO - Carmen Miranda 1939.ogg")

Composed by Assis Valente, recorded by Carmen Miranda in 1939

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/NoelRosa.gif/250px-NoelRosa.gif)](/wiki/File:NoelRosa.gif)

The sambista Noel Rosa was the first major figure in samba to bring the genre closer to the Brazilian middle class.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Carmen_Miranda_%281944%29.jpg/250px-Carmen_Miranda_%281944%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carmen_Miranda_(1944).jpg)

Carmen Miranda was the first samba singer to promote the genre internationally.

The 1930s in Brazilian music marked the rise of Estácio's samba as a musical genre to the detriment of maxixe-style samba.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro201571-185) If the samba schools were crucial to delimit, publicize and legitimize the new Estaciano samba as the authentic expression of the Rio's urban samba, the radio also played a decisive role in popularizing it nationwide.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200938-32)

Although broadcasting in Brazil was officially inaugurated in 1922,[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVirgilio2012-186) it was still an incipient and technical, experimental and restricted telecommunication medium.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos2005207–210-187) In the 1920s, Rio de Janeiro was home to only two short-range [radio stations](/wiki/Radio_broadcasting "Radio broadcasting")[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERuiz198497-188)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015281-189) whose programming was basically limited to broadcast educational content or classical music.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977639-190) This panorama changed radically in the 1930s, with the political rise of [Getúlio Vargas](/wiki/Get%C3%BAlio_Vargas "Getúlio Vargas"), who identified the media as a tool of public interest for economic, educational, cultural or political purposes, as well as for the national integration of the country.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos2005207–210-187)

A 1932 Vargas decree regulating radio advertising was crucial to the commercial, professional and popular transformation of Brazilian broadcasting.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos2005207–210-187)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERádios_EBC2015a-191) With the authorization that ads could occupy 20% (and then 25%) of the programming,[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERádios_EBC2015a-191) the radio became more attractive and safe for advertisers[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201567–69-192) and – added to the increase in sales of [radio sets](/wiki/Antique_radio "Antique radio") in the period – transformed this telecommunication medium of its function once educational for an entertainment powerhouse.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200977-193) With the contribution of financial resources from advertising, the broadcasters began to invest in musical programming, turning the radio into the great popularizer of popular music in the Brazil[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015235-33) – whether [phonograph record](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") or [live recordings](/wiki/Live_radio "Live radio") directly from the stations' auditoriums and studios.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977640-194) With samba as a great attraction, the radio gave space to the genre with the "sambas de carnaval", released for the carnival celebrations, and the "sambas de meio de ano" ("mid-year sambas"), launched throughout the year.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoura1983157-195)

This expansion of radio as a medium of [mass communication](/wiki/Mass_communication "Mass communication") enabled the formation of professional technicians linked to sound activities, as well as for singers, arrangers and composers.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoura1983157-195) From this scenario, broadcasters Ademar Casé (in Rio) and César Ladeira[[nb 9]](#cite_note-197) (in São Paulo) stood out as pioneers in the establishment of exclusive contracts with singers for presentation in live programs.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201567–69-192)[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrota2003102-198) That is, instead of receiving only one fee per presentation, the monthly remuneration was fixed to pay the artists, a model that triggered a fierce dispute between radio stations to form its professional and exclusive casts with popular stars of [Brazilian music](/wiki/Music_of_Brazil "Music of Brazil") and also [philharmonic orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra").[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201567–69-192)[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerraretto2001105-199) The most important samba singers, such as [Carmen Miranda](/wiki/Carmen_Miranda "Carmen Miranda"), started signing advantageous contracts to work exclusively with a certain radio station.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia200440-200)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOESP1955-201) The institution of auditorium programs created the need to set up big radio orchestras, conducted by arranging conductors, which gave a more sophisticated look to Brazilian popular music.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977640-194) One of the most notorious orchestral formations on the radio was the Orquestra Brasileira – under the command of conductor [Radamés Gnatalli](/wiki/Radam%C3%A9s_Gnattali "Radamés Gnattali") and with a team of musicians such as the sambistas João da Baiana, [Bide](/wiki/Alceb%C3%ADades_Barcelos "Alcebíades Barcelos") and [Heitor dos Prazeres](/wiki/Heitor_dos_Prazeres "Heitor dos Prazeres") in percussion[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOnofre201148-202) -, which combined standards of the international song at that time with popular instruments in Brazilian music, such as the cavaquinho.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaroldiMoreira200562-203)[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELisboa_Garcia2016-204) The Orquestra Brasileira was notable for the success of the program *Um milhão de melodias (One million melodies*), by [Rádio Nacional](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Nacional "Rádio Nacional"), one of the most popular in the history of Brazilian radio.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaroldiMoreira200560–61-205)

In this golden age of radio broadcasting in Brazil, a new generation of composers from the middle class emerged, such as [Ary Barroso](/wiki/Ary_Barroso "Ary Barroso"), [Ataulfo Alves](/wiki/Ataulfo_Alves "Ataulfo Alves"), [Braguinha](/wiki/Braguinha_(composer) "Braguinha (composer)"), [Lamartine Babo](/wiki/Lamartine_Babo "Lamartine Babo") and [Noel Rosa](/wiki/Noel_Rosa "Noel Rosa"), who have built successful careers in this media.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015100,_182-206) Grown up in the [Vila Isabel](/wiki/Vila_Isabel "Vila Isabel") middle-class neighborhood, Noel Rosa was instrumental in destigmatizing the samba do Estácio.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015124-141) Although he started his musical trajectory by composing Northeastern emboladas and similar Brazilian rural music genres, the composer changed his style by having contact with the samba made and sung by the sambistas from Estácio and others hills of Rio.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201582–83-207) This meeting resulted in friendships and partnerships between Noel and names as [Ismael Silva](/wiki/Ismael_Silva_(musician) "Ismael Silva (musician)") and [Cartola](/wiki/Cartola "Cartola").[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201582–83-207) Among singers, in addition to Noel himself, a new generation of performers broke out, such as Jonjoca, [Castro Barbosa](/wiki/Castro_Barbosa "Castro Barbosa"), [Luís Barbosa](/w/index.php?title=Lu%C3%ADs_Barbosa_(musician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luís Barbosa (musician) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Barbosa_(m%C3%BAsico) "pt:Luís Barbosa (músico)")], [Cyro Monteiro](/w/index.php?title=Cyro_Monteiro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cyro Monteiro (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyro_Monteiro "pt:Cyro Monteiro")], Dilermando Pinheiro, [Aracy de Almeida](/wiki/Aracy_de_Almeida "Aracy de Almeida"), [Marília Batista](/w/index.php?title=Mar%C3%ADlia_Batista&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marília Batista (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mar%C3%ADlia_Batista "pt:Marília Batista")].[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201553-122) Another highlight was the singer Carmen Miranda, the greatest star of Brazilian popular music at that time and the first artist to promote samba internationally.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019114-171)[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977489–490-208) Renowned in Brazil, Carmen continued her successful artistic career in the United States, where she worked in musicals in New York City and, later, in [Hollywood cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977489–490-208) Her popularity was such that she even performed at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") for President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977489–490-208)

The consolidation of samba as the flagship of the radio programming of Rio de Janeiro was characterized by the association of the musical genre with the image of white artists, who, even when proletarianized, were more palatable to the preference of the public, while the poor black sambistas remained normally on the sidelines of this process as a mere supplier of compositions for the white performers or as instrumentalists accompanying them.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFenerick2002191–192-209) This strong presence of white singers and composers was also decisive for the acceptance and appreciation of samba by the economic and cultural elites of Brazil.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015183-40)[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaboiaMartini2016a-210)[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenevides2004-211) From this, the middle class started to recognize the value of the rhythm invented by black Brazilians.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaboiaMartini2016a-210) The [Municipal Theater of Rio](/wiki/Theatro_Municipal_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Theatro Municipal (Rio de Janeiro)") became the stage for elegant carnival balls attended by the high society.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlmeida201363–67-212) Having contact with the popular genre through samba and [choro](/wiki/Choro "Choro") circles meetings,[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977376-213) the renowned conductor [Heitor Villa-Lobos](/wiki/Heitor_Villa-Lobos "Heitor Villa-Lobos") promoted a musical meeting between the American maestro [Leopold Stokowski](/wiki/Leopold_Stokowski "Leopold Stokowski") with the sambistas Cartola, Zé da Zilda, Zé Espinguela, [Donga](/wiki/Donga_(musician) "Donga (musician)"), [João da Baiana](/w/index.php?title=Jo%C3%A3o_da_Baiana&action=edit&redlink=1 "João da Baiana (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_da_Baiana "pt:João da Baiana")] and others.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214) The recording results were edited in the United States on several 78 rpm discs.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214)[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977237-215) Another privileged space for the white, rich elite in the Brazilian society was the [casinos](/wiki/Casino "Casino"), which peaked in Brazil during the 1930s and 1940s.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAgência_Senado2016-216)[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarazzai2016-217) In addition to working with [games of chance](/wiki/Game_of_chance "Game of chance"), these elegant amusement houses offered restaurant and bar services and were the stage for shows – among which samba also featured prominently.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarazzai2016-217)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201557-218) Thus, the casinos signed exclusive contracts with major artists, as was the case with Carmen Miranda as a big star at Cassino da Urca.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAgência_Senado2016-216)[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarazzai2016-217) In an unusual event for the universe of sambistas on the hill, composer Cartola performed for a month at the luxurious Casino Atlântico, in [Copacabana](/wiki/Copacabana,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Copacabana, Rio de Janeiro"), in 1940.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214)

The consolidation of samba among Brazilian elites was also influenced by the valorization of the ideology of [miscegenation](/wiki/Miscegenation "Miscegenation") in vogue with the construction of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") under the [Getulio Vargas regime](/wiki/Vargas_Era "Vargas Era").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200974-219) From an image of a symbol of national backwardness, the [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") became a representative of Brazilian singularities, and samba, with its mestizo origin, ended up linked to the construction of [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity").[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaiva200974–75-220)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockler20116-38) Having acted decisively for the growth of radio in Brazil, the Vargas government perceived samba as a vital element in the construction of this idea of miscegenation.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015150-167)[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaboiaMartini2016a-210)[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira2012108-221) Samba's triumph over the airwaves allowed it to penetrate all sectors of Brazilian society.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilman2001-222)

Especially under the [Estado Novo](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Brazil) "Estado Novo (Brazil)"), whose ideological cultural policy of reconceptualizing the popular and extolling everything that was considered an authentic national expression,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015182-4)[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1982103-223) samba was elevated to the position of major national symbol of the country[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos2003109-13)[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201517–18-224)[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesar_Carvalho2004-225)[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVicenteDeMarchiGambaro2016461–464-226) and the official pace of the country.[[nb 10]](#cite_note-228) However, one of the concerns of the Vargas regime was to interfere in music production to promote samba as a means of "pedagogical" socialization,[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeirelles19429-229) that is, by banning compositions that confront the regime's ethics.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso198730–35-230) In this quest to "civilize" samba,[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalgado194179–93-231) political bodies such as the Department of Press and Propaganda (DIP, *Departamento de Imprensa e Propaganda*) took action to order sambas that would exalt the work and censor lyrics that addressed bohemia and *malandragem*,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015183-40)[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso198730–35-230) two of the most common themes in the tradition of the urban Carioca samba.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERibeiro201010-232)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes197786-233) Musical contests were also instituted through which public opinion elected its favorite composers and performers.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso198722-234)

Under Vargas, samba had an expressive weight in the construction of an image of Brazil abroad and was an important means of cultural and tourist dissemination of the country.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso1982103-223) In an attempt to reinforce a positive national image, the presence of renowned singers of the kind in presidential committees to Latin American countries has become frequent.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1990236–237-235)[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParanhos20129-236) At the end of 1937, the sambistas [Paulo da Portela](/w/index.php?title=Paulo_da_Portela&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paulo da Portela (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paulo_da_Portela "pt:Paulo da Portela")] and Heitor dos Prazeres participated in a caravan of Brazilian artists to [Montevideo](/wiki/Montevideo "Montevideo") that performed at the Gran Exposición Feria Internacional del Uruguay.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214) The Brazilian government also financed an information and popular music program called "Uma Hora do Brasil", produced and broadcast by Radio El Mundo, from [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), which had at least one broadcast to [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1990236–237-235) When the Vargas regime approached the United States, DIP made an agreement to broadcast Brazilian radio programs on hundreds of [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") radio network.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoulart199069-237) Under this context, the samba "[Aquarela do Brasil](/wiki/Aquarela_do_Brasil "Aquarela do Brasil")" (by Ary Barroso) was released in the United States market,[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes197775–78-238) becoming the first Brazilian song that was very successful abroad[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerreira2020a-239)[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanucci2019-240) and one of the most popular works of the Brazilian popular songbook.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano1997178-241) In the midst of the good neighborhood policy, the animator [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") visited Portela samba school during his visit to Brazil in 1941, from which he hypothesized that [Zé Carioca](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Carioca "José Carioca"), a character created by the cartoonist to express the Brazilian way,[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVelloso198722-234) would have been inspired by the figure of the sambista Paulo da Portela.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214)

The rise of samba as a popular musical genre in Brazil also relied on its dissemination in [Brazilian cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_Brazil "Cinema of Brazil"), especially in musical comedies, being an integral part of the soundtrack, the plot or even the main theme of the cinematographic work.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchvarzman20061–9-242)[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201563–64-243) The good public acceptance of the short film "A Voz do Carnaval" (by [Adhemar Gonzaga](/wiki/Adhemar_Gonzaga "Adhemar Gonzaga")) paved the way for several other cinematographic works related to rhythm,[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201563–64-243)[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEECB1997130–131-244) many of which had a strong presence of radio idol singers in the cast, such as "Alô, Alô, Brasil!", which had sisters Carmen and [Aurora Miranda](/wiki/Aurora_Miranda "Aurora Miranda"), Francisco Alves, Mário Reis, [Dircinha Batista](/wiki/Dircinha_Batista "Dircinha Batista"), [Bando da Lua](/wiki/Bando_da_Lua "Bando da Lua"), Almirante, Lamartine Babo, among others.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchvarzman20061–9-242) The advent of the popular chanchada films made Brazilian cinema one of the biggest promoters of carnival music.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEECB1997117–119-245)[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201564-246) In one of the rare moments when sambistas from the hill starred in radio programs, Paulo da Portela, Heitor dos Prazeres and Cartola led the program "A Voz do Morro", at Rádio Cruzeiro do Sul, in 1941.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214) There, they presented unpublished sambas whose titles were given by listeners.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977160–162,_592–593,_625–627-247) However, over the course of the decade, the samba made by these genuine sambistas was losing space on Brazilian radio to new sub-genres that were being formed, while figures such as Cartola and Ismael Silva were ostracized until they left the music scene in the late 1940s.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019114-171)

### New sub-genres of samba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: New sub-genres of samba")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Ary_Barroso_1939.jpg/250px-Ary_Barroso_1939.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ary_Barroso_1939.jpg)

Ary Barroso became one of the major composers of samba in the "golden age" of Brazilian music.
