Thanks to its economic exploitation through the radio and the records, samba not only became professional,[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015229-248) but also diversified into new sub-genres,[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015149-249) many of which were different from the hues originating in the hills of Rio de Janeiro[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVagalume197829-179) and established by the interests of the Brazilian music industry.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015150-167) The period of Brazilian music between 1929 and 1945 marked by the arrival of radio and electromagnetic recording of sound in the country and by the notability of major composers and singers,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano1997241-42) – the so-called "golden age" registered several styles of samba, some with greater and others with less solidity.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013127-43)

Publications devoted to the topic disseminated a broad conceptual terminology, including denominations later enshrined in new sub-genres – such as [samba-canção](/wiki/Samba-can%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Samba-canção"), [samba-choro](/wiki/Samba-choro "Samba-choro"), [samba-enredo](/wiki/Samba-enredo "Samba-enredo"), [samba-exaltação](/w/index.php?title=Samba-exalta%C3%A7%C3%A3o&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samba-exaltação (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/samba-exalta%C3%A7%C3%A3o "pt:samba-exaltação")], [samba-de-terreiro](/w/index.php?title=Samba-de-terreiro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samba-de-terreiro (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/samba-de-terreiro "pt:samba-de-terreiro")], [samba de breque](/wiki/Samba_de_breque "Samba de breque") -, as well as registered [scores](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") and released labels and album covers printed various nomenclatures for samba in an attempt to express a functional, rhythmic or thematic trend – such as "samba à moda baiana" (samba in the Bahian style), "samba-batucada", "samba-jongo", "samba-maxixe" -, although some sounded quite inconsistent – such as "samba à moda agrião" (samba in the watercress style), "samba epistolar" (epistolary samba) and "samba fonético" (phonetic samba).[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013127-43) In other cases, it was music critics that imputed pejorative labels with a view to disapproving certain aesthetic changes or fashion trends – as in the disparagingly called sambalada and sambolero for stylistic nuances the samba-canção.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015269-44)

Established in the radio era as one of the main sub-genres of samba, the [samba-canção](/wiki/Samba-can%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Samba-canção") style emerged among professional musicians who played in the [revues](/wiki/Revue "Revue") of Rio de Janeiro in the late 1920s and early 1930s.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201755-250)[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013128-251) Although the term began to circulating in the press in 1929 to mistakenly designate "Jura", by Sinhô, and "Diz que me amas", by J. Machado,[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1974150-252) the starting point of the line was "Linda Flor (Ai, Ioiô)", a melody by Henrique Vogeler and lyrics by Luis Peixoto,[[nb 11]](#cite_note-254) released in the revue and on disc by singer [Aracy Cortes](/wiki/Aracy_Cortes "Aracy Cortes").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3) In general, the samba-canção was characterized as a slower tempo variant, with a dominance of the melodic line over the rhythmic marking[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETatit199623-255) that basically explores the subjectivity of subjectivity and feeling.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013129-256)[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015267-257) As their releases took place outside the carnival season, the trend was linked to the so-called "mid-year samba". However, during the 1930s, the term samba-canção was used arbitrarily to designate many compositions contained under the name of "samba de meio de ano" ("mid-year samba"),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3)[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201755-250) but which did not fit as samba-canção themselves.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013129-256)[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1974149-258) On the other hand, many sambas at the time of their releases would later be recognized as samba-canção, as in the case of works by [Noel Rosa](/wiki/Noel_Rosa "Noel Rosa") and [Ary Barroso](/wiki/Ary_Barroso "Ary Barroso").[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013130-259)[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro201572–73-260) Not by chance, Zuza Homem de Mello and Jairo Severiano consider that this samba style was truly inaugurated with the second version of the song "No rancho fundo", with melody by Ary Barroso and lyrics by [Lamartine Babo](/wiki/Lamartine_Babo "Lamartine Babo").[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano1997146-261)

Basically, Carnaval was reserved for the launch of [marchinhas](/wiki/Marchinha "Marchinha") and [sambas-enredo](/wiki/Samba-enredo "Samba-enredo"), a sub-genre typified in this way in the 1930s because of the lyrics and melody, which must comprise the poetic summary of the theme chosen by the samba school for its carnival parade.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015257-262) Samba-de-terreiro – or also samba de quadra – was a short-tempo samba modality, with the second most measured part that prepares the [bateria](/wiki/Bateria "Bateria") for a more lively return to the beginning.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684–685-263)[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015287-264) Its format was also consolidated in the 1930s.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015287-264)

Also from that time, [samba-choro](/wiki/Samba-choro "Samba-choro") – at first called choro-canção or choro-cantado – was a syncopated hybrid sub-genre of samba with the instrumental music genre [choro](/wiki/Choro "Choro"), but with medium tempo and presence of lyrics.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015267-257) Created by the Brazilian music industry, it was released, with all indications, with "Amor em excesso", by Gadé and Valfrido Silva, in 1932.[[nb 12]](#cite_note-265)[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrasil_Silva2008123-266) One of the most popular sambas of this variant is "Carinhoso", by [Pixinguinha](/wiki/Pixinguinha "Pixinguinha"), released as choro in 1917, received lyrics and ended up relaunched two decades later, in the voice of Orlando Silva, with great commercial success.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019114-171) In the following decade, [Waldir Azevedo](/wiki/Valdir_Azevedo "Valdir Azevedo") would popularize chorinho, a kind of fast-moving instrumental samba.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019114-171)

Widespread during the Estado Novo, samba-exaltação was a sub-genre marked by the character of grandeur, expressed notably by the extensive melody, the lyrics with a patriotic-ufanist theme and by the lavish orchestral arrangement.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684–685-263)[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015268-267) Its great paradigm was "[Aquarela do Brasil](/wiki/Aquarela_do_Brasil "Aquarela do Brasil")", by [Ary Barroso](/wiki/Ary_Barroso "Ary Barroso").[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira2012114-268)[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerreira2019-269) From the huge success of the first version recorded by [Francisco Alves](/wiki/Francisco_Alves_(singer) "Francisco Alves (singer)"), in 1939, samba-exaltação started to be well cultivated by professional composers in the musical theater and in the music industry and radio media.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684–685-263) Another well-known samba of this type was "Brasil Pandeiro", by Assis Valente, a huge hit with the vocal group Anjos do Inferno in 1941.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015125-214)

At the turn of the 1940s, [samba de breque](/wiki/Samba_de_breque "Samba de breque") emerged, a sub-genre marked by a markedly syncopated rhythm and sudden stops called *breques* (from English word *break*, Brazilian term for [car brakes](/wiki/Brake "Brake")),[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPortes201481-270) to which the singer added spoken comments, generally humorous in character, alluding to the theme.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977684-3)[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015256-271) The singer [Moreira da Silva](/wiki/Moreira_da_Silva "Moreira da Silva") consolidated himself as the great name of this sub-genre.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETatit2000-272)[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPortes201417-273)

### Samba-canção hegemony and influences of foreign music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Samba-canção hegemony and influences of foreign music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Lupic%C3%ADnio_Rodrigues_%281968%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Lupic%C3%ADnio_Rodrigues_%281968%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lupic%C3%ADnio_Rodrigues_(1968).tif)

The composer Lupicínio Rodrigues, author of some of the great classics of the samba-canção style.

After the end of the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and the consequent growth in the production of consumer goods, radio sets spread in the Brazilian market in different models and at affordable prices to the different social class of the Brazilian population.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalabre200212-274) Within this context, Brazilian radio broadcasting also went through a moment of change in language and audience[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201064-275) that made radio an even more popular media in Brazil.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalabre200212-274) In search of easier communication with the listener, the programming standard became more sensational, melodramatic and appealing.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201065-276) One of the best expressions of this new format and the new popular audience was the auditorium programs and the "kings" and "radio queen" contests.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalabre200212-274)[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201065-276) Although they played a role in legitimizing samba as a cultural product and national symbol music[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201546,_75–79-277) and also transforming popular musical culture with the circulation of new musical genres and more extroverted performances,[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201065-276) auditorium programs such as the paradigmatic "Programa César de Alencar" and "Programa Manoel Barcelos"[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERádios_EBC2015b-278) – both on [Radio Nacional](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Nacional "Rádio Nacional"), leader in audience and main media of communication in Brazil[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201511,_81-279) – stimulated the cult of personality and the private life of artists,[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201065-276) whose apex was the collective frenzy generated around the fan clubs of popular music stars during the concourses of kings and queens of the radio.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitanoWasserman2000167–189-280)[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201594-281)

For the samba more linked to the traditions of Estácio and the hills, the 1950s was characterized by the vitalizing presence of old and new composers who led the renewal of the genre for the next years.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201067-282)[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximo2016-283) This renewal was present in the sambas of well-known authors from the general public, such as Geraldo Pereira[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes197785–89-284) and [Wilson Batista](/w/index.php?title=Wilson_Batista&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wilson Batista (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilson_Batista "pt:Wilson Batista")],[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977602–603-285) of lesser-known sambistas but active in their communities, such as [Zé Keti](/wiki/Z%C3%A9_Keti "Zé Keti")[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977822–823-286) and [Nelson Cavaquinho](/wiki/Nelson_Cavaquinho "Nelson Cavaquinho")[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977524–526-287) – a composer who would establish a great partnership with [Guilherme de Brito](/wiki/Guilherme_de_Brito "Guilherme de Brito")[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201067-282) – and also of new composers, such as [Monsueto](/wiki/Monsueto "Monsueto").[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019115-288) The samba de breque by [Jorge Veiga](/w/index.php?title=Jorge_Veiga&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jorge Veiga (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jorge_Veiga "pt:Jorge Veiga")] also stood out[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977785–786-289) and, in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), the [Demônios da Garoa](/wiki/Dem%C3%B4nios_da_Garoa "Demônios da Garoa") enshrined the sambas by [Adoniran Barbosa](/wiki/Adoniran_Barbosa "Adoniran Barbosa").[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201067-282) Missing for many years, samba composer [Cartola](/wiki/Cartola "Cartola") was found washing cars in [Ipanema](/wiki/Ipanema "Ipanema") by journalist [Sérgio Porto](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Porto "Sérgio Porto"), who took him to sing on [Rádio Mayrink Veiga](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Mayrink_Veiga "Rádio Mayrink Veiga") and got him a job at a newspaper.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977160–162-290) As part of the celebrations of the Fourth Centenary of the city of São Paulo, the composer Almirante organized the "Festival da Velha Guarda" ("Old Guard Festival"),[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201066-291) which brought together great names of Brazilian popular music then forgotten, such as [Donga](/wiki/Donga_(musician) "Donga (musician)"), [Ismael Silva](/wiki/Ismael_Silva_(musician) "Ismael Silva (musician)"), and [Pixinguinha](/wiki/Pixinguinha "Pixinguinha").[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes197720–21-292)

However, the period between the second half of the 1940s and the end of the 1950s – well known as [post-war](/wiki/Post-war "Post-war") – was deeply characterized by the prestige and dominance of samba-canção in the Brazilian music scene.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013130-259)[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeveriano2009273-293) Although in its time of appearance there were not so many releases characteristic of this aspect, many achieved huge commercial success and,[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201756-294) in the mid-1940s,[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201756-294) this sub-genre began to dominate Brazilian radio programming[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013130-259) and be the most played style outside the carnival era.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro201572-295) This rise of samba-canção as a hegemonic musical style was also accompanied mainly by the avalanche of foreign musical genres[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbin2003142-296)[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977685-297) – imported to Brazil under the political-cultural context of World War II[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão199760-298)[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996b85-299) – that began to compete in the country's market with the samba-canção itself.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitanoWasserman2000167–189-280)[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximo2016-283) [Tango](/wiki/Tango "Tango") and, especially, [bolero](/wiki/Bolero "Bolero"), which occupied a significant part of radio programming, proliferated in clubs and dance halls in Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201757-300) [Music from the United States](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_States "Music of the United States") has also come to occupy a large part of the programming of Brazilian radio stations.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996b92-301) With [big bands](/wiki/Big_band "Big band") in evidence, some radio stations made great publicity about [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), a genre that was gaining more and more appreciation among some musicians from Rio de Janeiro, especially those who worked in [nightclubs](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub").[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitanoWasserman2000167–189-280) In a samba-canção rhythm, many boleros, [foxtrots](/wiki/Foxtrot "Foxtrot") and [French songs](/wiki/Music_of_France "Music of France") were also part of the repertoire of nightclub pianists.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro201572–73-260)

Under the influence of the strong penetration of these imported genres, the post-war samba-canção itself was influenced by these rhythms.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013130-259) In certain cases, the change occurred through a musical treatment based on the [cool jazz](/wiki/Cool_jazz "Cool jazz") tones and more restrained vocal performances, and more complex melodic-harmonic structures,[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia199940-302) distinct, therefore, from the rhythmic-bodily sensuality of traditional samba.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201067-282)[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVargasBruck2017234-303) In other cases, it was due to the strong passionate exercised by bolero[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015150-167)[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia199940-302) and foreign [sentimental ballads](/wiki/Sentimental_ballad "Sentimental ballad").[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitanoWasserman2000167–189-280) Both influences displeased the more traditionalist critics: in the first, they accused the samba-canção of having "jazzed up",[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201066-291) especially for the sophisticated orchestra arrangements;[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximo2016-283) in the second, the slower and more romantic progress of the slope led to pejorative labels such as "sambolero" or "sambalada".[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015269-44)[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977685-297) In fact, the orchestral accompaniments of the samba-canção at that time were marked by arrangements containing woodwinds and strings that replaced the traditional regional musical ensemble[[nb 13]](#cite_note-305) and made it possible to dramatize the arrangements in accordance with the theme of the songs and the expressiveness of the singers.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaves200410-306) If, for some critics, these orchestral and melodic-harmonic attributes of modern 1950s samba-canção came from post-war [American culture](/wiki/American_imperialism "American imperialism"),[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão199753-307) for others this influence was much more Latin American than North American.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbin2003153-308) Another aesthetic mark of the period was the vocal performance of the singers of this style of samba,[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaves200411-309) sometimes more inclined to the lyrical power and expressiveness, sometimes more supported by an intonation and close to the colloquial dynamics.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201763-310)

With a new generation of performers that emerged in the post-war period, the Brazilian music scene was taken over by emotional and painful samba-canção songs in the 1950s.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201760-311)[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECabral1996b97-312)[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeveriano2009292-313)[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaves200412-314) This sub-genre was divided between a more traditional and a more modern generation.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano1997241-42)[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201764-315) If in the first group there were composers such as [Lupicínio Rodrigues](/wiki/Lupic%C3%ADnio_Rodrigues "Lupicínio Rodrigues") and [Herivelto Martins](/wiki/Herivelto_Martins "Herivelto Martins") and interpreters such as [Nelson Gonçalves](/wiki/Nelson_Gon%C3%A7alves "Nelson Gonçalves"), [Dalva de Oliveira](/wiki/Dalva_de_Oliveira "Dalva de Oliveira"), [Angela Maria](/wiki/Angela_Maria "Angela Maria"), [Jamelão](/wiki/Jamel%C3%A3o "Jamelão"), [Cauby Peixoto](/wiki/Cauby_Peixoto "Cauby Peixoto") and [Elizeth Cardoso](/wiki/Elizete_Cardoso "Elizete Cardoso"), the second group had as main exponents [Dick Farney](/wiki/Dick_Farney "Dick Farney"), Lúcio Alves, [Tito Madi](/wiki/Tito_Madi "Tito Madi"), [Nora Ney](/wiki/Nora_Ney "Nora Ney"), [Dolores Duran](/wiki/Dolores_Duran "Dolores Duran"), [Maysa](/wiki/Maysa_Matarazzo "Maysa Matarazzo") and [Sylvia Telles](/wiki/Sylvia_Telles "Sylvia Telles"), among others.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzalez201764-315)[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeveriano2009291-316) The modern samba-canção was also part of a phase of [Dorival Caymmi](/wiki/Dorival_Caymmi "Dorival Caymmi")'s career[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximo2018-317)[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerreira2020b-318) and the beginning of the musical work of [Antonio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim "Antônio Carlos Jobim"),[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2013130-259)[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2015358-319) one of the great names of the new style of samba that would stylistically mark the genre and Brazilian music in the coming years.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano201067-282)

### Bossa nova, the new revolution in samba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bossa nova, the new revolution in samba")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto_e_Stan_Getz_em_Nova_York_1972_cr.jpg/250px-Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto_e_Stan_Getz_em_Nova_York_1972_cr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto_e_Stan_Getz_em_Nova_York_1972_cr.jpg)

The composer João Gilberto, an originator of bossa nova.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Tom_Jobim%2C_1965.jpg/250px-Tom_Jobim%2C_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tom_Jobim,_1965.jpg)

The composer Antonio Carlos Jobim, author of classic bossa nova sambas.

The period between [Juscelino Kubitschek](/wiki/Juscelino_Kubitschek "Juscelino Kubitschek")'s inauguration in 1956, until the political crisis in the [João Goulart](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Goulart "João Goulart") government that culminated in the [1964 Brazilian coup d'état](/wiki/1964_Brazilian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1964 Brazilian coup d'état"), was characterized by great effervescence on the Brazilian music scene, especially in Rio de Janeiro.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry2015100-320) Although it lost its status as the country's capital after the inauguration of [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília"), the city maintained its position as a major cultural hub in the country and urban samba,[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry2015100-320) whose transformations on the radio, the music industry, nightclubs and among the circles of university middle class youth resulted in [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova")[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201546-321) – a term by which a new style of rhythmic accompaniment and interpretation of samba spread from the [South Zone of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/South_Zone_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "South Zone (Rio de Janeiro)") became known.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitanoWasserman2000167–189-280)[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015255-322)

At a time when the appeal to the traditional was gaining new momentum, bossa nova would mark the entire structure of creation and listening supported by established genres, considering that it sought a renewal within the tradition of samba.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitanoWasserman2000167–189-280) Initially called "modern samba" by the Brazilian music critic,[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015266-323) this new sub-genre was officially inaugurated with the composition "[Chega de Saudade](/wiki/Chega_de_Saudade "Chega de Saudade")", by [Antonio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim "Antônio Carlos Jobim") and [Vinicius de Moraes](/wiki/Vinicius_de_Moraes "Vinicius de Moraes"), released in 1958 in two versions: one sung by [Elizeth Cardoso](/wiki/Elizete_Cardoso "Elizete Cardoso")[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelito2018-324)[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBernardo2019-325) and the other with the singer, songwriter, and guitarist [João Gilberto](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto "João Gilberto").[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2018-326)[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEstadão_Conteúdo2018-327)[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-328)[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia2008-329) A Bahian-born living in Rio, Gilberto radically changed the way of interpreting samba until then, changing the harmonies with the introduction of unconventional [guitar chords](/wiki/Guitar_chord "Guitar chord") and revolutionizing the classic syncope of the genre with a unique rhythmic division.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201546-321)[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-328) These formal Gilbertian experiences were consolidated in the studio album [Chega de Saudade](/wiki/Chega_de_Saudade_(album) "Chega de Saudade (album)"), released in 1959,[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBernardo2019-325) and triggered the emergence of an artistic movement around Gilberto and others professional artists such as Antonio Carlos Jobim, Vinicius de Moraes, [Baden Powell](/wiki/Baden_Powell_(guitarist) "Baden Powell (guitarist)"), [Alaíde Costa](/wiki/Ala%C3%ADde_Costa "Alaíde Costa"), [Sylvia Telles](/wiki/Sylvia_Telles "Sylvia Telles"), among others, which attracted young amateur musicians from the South Zone of Rio – almost all from the middle class and with university degrees[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201546-321) – such as [Carlos Lyra](/wiki/Carlos_Lyra "Carlos Lyra"), [Roberto Menescal](/wiki/Roberto_Menescal "Roberto Menescal"), [Ronaldo Boscoli](/wiki/Ronaldo_B%C3%B4scoli "Ronaldo Bôscoli") and [Nara Leão](/wiki/Nara_Le%C3%A3o "Nara Leão").[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2018-326)[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977106–107-330)

Consolidated in the following years as a type of concert samba, non-dancing, and comparable to American [cool jazz](/wiki/Cool_jazz "Cool jazz"),[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019115-288) bossa nova has become a sambistic sub-genre of great reputation on the Brazilian music scene and, with its rhythm, more assimilable abroad than traditional samba, became known worldwide.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximo2016-283)[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977106–107-330)[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrange2018-331) After being released on the American market in a series of concerts in New York City in late 1962,[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-328)[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEntini2012-332)[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMenezes2017-333)[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartínez2018-334) Brazilian bossa nova albums [were reissued](/wiki/Reissue "Reissue") in several countries, while new songs and albums were recorded, including with foreign artists.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977106–107-330) Several of these works – with the samba "[The Girl from Ipanema](/wiki/The_Girl_from_Ipanema "The Girl from Ipanema")", by Antonio Carlos Jobim and Vinicius de Moraes, at the frontline[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-328)[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEG12012-335)[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERevista_Veja2012-336) – became major international successes.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMáximo2016-283) However, in the midst of the turbulence that marked the Brazilian political scene at the time, the movement suffered a dissent, which resulted in the so-called "nationalist current".[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerolamo2017172–198-337) With the intention of carrying out a work more engaged and aligned with the social context of the period,[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerolamo2017172–198-337) the nationalist bossa-novistas formed around Nara Leão, Carlos Lyra, [Sérgio Ricardo](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Ricardo_(director) "Sérgio Ricardo (director)"), [Edu Lobo](/wiki/Edu_Lobo "Edu Lobo"), and the partnership between Vinicius de Moraes and Baden Powell, the latter two signing a fertile partnership that resulted in the studio album "[Os Afro-sambas](/wiki/Os_Afro-sambas "Os Afro-sambas")", with positive international impact.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201519–20-338)

In addition to bossa nova, other new samba sub-genres emerged in this period between the late 1950s and early 1960s. The rise of nightclubs as the main nightlife venues in Rio disseminated variety shows with the participation of sambistas and samba dancers,[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019114-171) mainly performed by [instrumental musical ensemble](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble") with keyboard, electric guitar, [acoustic bass guitar](/wiki/Acoustic_bass_guitar "Acoustic bass guitar"), drums and percussion, and performed by crooners.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201542-339) A trend in the 1960s live music in Brazil, this format of "samba to dance" resulted in styles such as the [sambalanço](/wiki/Sambalan%C3%A7o "Sambalanço") – a very lively and dancing type of samba, from which musicians such as [Ed Lincoln](/wiki/Ed_Lincoln "Ed Lincoln") and performers such as [Sílvio César](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%ADlvio_C%C3%A9sar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sílvio César (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%C3%ADlvio_C%C3%A9sar "pt:Sílvio César")], [Pedrinho Rodrigues](/w/index.php?title=Pedrinho_Rodrigues&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pedrinho Rodrigues (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pedrinho_Rodrigues "pt:Pedrinho Rodrigues")], [Orlandivo](/w/index.php?title=Orlandivo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Orlandivo (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orlandivo "pt:Orlandivo")], [Miltinho](/wiki/Miltinho "Miltinho") and [Elza Soares](/wiki/Elza_Soares "Elza Soares") stood out.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza201031–32-340)[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201543-341) In this same environment, [samba-jazz](/wiki/Samba-jazz "Samba-jazz") also emerged, consolidated with the success of bossa nova that brought samba and [bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") closer together,[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGomes201084-342) initially based on the piano-bass-drums musical ensemble[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGomes201062-343) and later broader formations.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015268-267) Also under this context, the composer [Jorge Ben](/wiki/Jorge_Ben "Jorge Ben") emerged with his peculiar and hybrid way of playing samba, mixing elements of bossa nova and American [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [rock'n'roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll")[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPareles1986-344)[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENascimento20078–9-345)[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENascimento200867,_114-346) that would even take samba songs such as "[Mas que Nada](/wiki/Mas_que_Nada "Mas que Nada")" and "Chove Chuva", released by [Sérgio Mendes & Brazil '66](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Mendes#Brasil_'66 "Sérgio Mendes"), to the [Billboard charts](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts").[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillboard1966-347)[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillboard1967-348) And at the end of the 1960s, [samba funk](/wiki/Samba_funk "Samba funk") emerged, led by pianist [Dom Salvador](/wiki/Dom_Salvador "Dom Salvador"), which mixed the [two beats to the bar](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)") of samba and the four beats to the bar of American [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") that had just arrived in the Brazilian music market at that time.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKassel2018-349)

The period was also characterized by the profusion of some [partner dance](/wiki/Partner_dance "Partner dance") samba styles.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019116-350) These were the cases of [Samba de Gafieira](/wiki/Samba_de_Gafieira "Samba de Gafieira"), a dance style developed in the [ballroom dance](/wiki/Ballroom_dance "Ballroom dance") of suburban clubs in Rio de Janeiro frequented by people with low purchasing power throughout the 1940s and 1950s and which also became a fad among upper-middle-class people in the 1960s,[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015301–302-351)[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielmann2016627-352) and the [samba rock](/wiki/Samba_rock "Samba rock"), a dance style born in the São Paulo suburban parties in the 1960s, mixing steps from samba, rock and [Caribbean rhythms](/wiki/List_of_Caribbean_music_genres "List of Caribbean music genres") such as [rumba](/wiki/Rumba "Rumba") and [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music").[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacedo200718-353)[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOliveira20062–10-354)[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015270-355) The "bailes blacks" ("black balls") experienced their peak notably in Rio and São Paulo in the 1970s, a time of great diffusion of the [American black music](/wiki/Black_music "Black music") in Brazil, which were frequently disseminated at these "bailes blacks".[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerraz201842–43-356)[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlberto201655–56-357) This also generated a new debate among the Brazilian music critic about the foreign influence on Brazilian music and also on samba itself.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerraz201820-358)

### Traditional samba as "resistance music"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Traditional samba as \"resistance music\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Cartola%2C_1970.jpg/250px-Cartola%2C_1970.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cartola,_1970.jpg)

The sambista Cartola experienced the resurgence of his musical career in the 1960s and 1970s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Clementina_de_Jesus_%281966%29.jpg/250px-Clementina_de_Jesus_%281966%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clementina_de_Jesus_(1966).jpg)

Clementina de Jesus recorded her first LP only at the age of 65.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Paulinho_da_Viola_e_Martinho_da_Vila.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Paulinho_da_Viola_e_Martinho_da_Vila.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paulinho_da_Viola_e_Martinho_da_Vila.tif)

Paulinho da Viola and Martinho da Vila started their musical careers in the 1960s.

In 1962, the "Carta do samba" ("The samba letter") was made public, a document written by the writer [Edison Carneiro](/wiki/Edison_Carneiro "Edison Carneiro")[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019129-359) that expressed the need to preserve traditional features of samba, such as the [syncopa](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation"), without, however, "denying or taking away spontaneity and prospects for progress".[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarneiro201224-360) This letter came to meet a series of circumstances that made traditional urban samba not only revalued in different Brazilian cultural circles, but also started to be considered by them as a kind of "counter-hegemonic" and "resistance music" in the Brazilian music scene.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry2015111-361) In a decade characterized in the Brazilian music industry by the domination of international rock music and its Brazilian variant, [Jovem Guarda](/wiki/Jovem_Guarda "Jovem Guarda"), the traditional samba would have started to be seen as an expression of the greatest authenticity and purity of the genre,[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015203-362) which led to the creation of terms such as "samba autêntico" ("authentic samba"), "samba de morro" ("samba of the hill"), "samba de raiz" ("roots samba"), or "samba de verdade" ("real samba").[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry2015111-361)

One of the major expressions of this "resistance samba" in the first half of the 1960s was Zicartola, a bar opened by sambista [Cartola](/wiki/Cartola "Cartola") and his wife Dona Zica in 1963.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015301–302-351)[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2016-363) which transformed in a short time at a famous meeting point of veteran sambistas, attracted the attendance of many left-wing intellectuals and students, and became famous for its samba nights that, in addition to revealing new talents, such as [Paulinho da Viola](/wiki/Paulinho_da_Viola "Paulinho da Viola"), revived the careers of former composers then ostracized from the music industry.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977160–162-290)[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015301–302-351) In February 1964, the year of the [Brazilian military coup d'état](/wiki/1964_Brazilian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1964 Brazilian coup d'état"), [Nara Leão](/wiki/Nara_Le%C3%A3o "Nara Leão")'s debut album was released, which included sambas by traditional samba composers such as Cartola, [Elton Medeiros](/w/index.php?title=Elton_Medeiros&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elton Medeiros (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elton_Medeiros "pt:Elton Medeiros")], [Nelson Cavaquinho](/wiki/Nelson_Cavaquinho "Nelson Cavaquinho") and Zé Keti, as well as samba songs from the bossa nova nationalist branch.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015151-364) And at the end of that year, Nara Leão met with Zé Keti and João do Vale for the musical Show Opinião, which became a reference as an artistic manifestation in protest to the authoritarian regime established.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015103-365)[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia201810-366)[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESá2014-367)

The following year, the composer Hermínio Bello de Carvalho produced Rosa de Ouro, a musical that launched the sixty-year-old [Clementina de Jesus](/wiki/Clementina_de_Jesus "Clementina de Jesus") to the general public.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENuzzi2017-368)[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva201134-369) It was the birth of the professional artistic career of one of the most expressive voices in the samba history,[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015103-365) characterized by a repertoire aimed at the African music matrixes, such as [jongos](/wiki/Jongo "Jongo"), [curimbas](/wiki/Umbanda "Umbanda"), [lundus](/wiki/Lundu_(dance) "Lundu (dance)") and sambas of the rural tradition.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201533-370) The music ensemble to accompany Clementina in this show was composed by Paulinho da Viola, Elton Medeiros, [Anescarzinho do Salgueiro](/w/index.php?title=Anescarzinho_do_Salgueiro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anescarzinho do Salgueiro (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anescarzinho_do_Salgueiro "pt:Anescarzinho do Salgueiro")], Jair do Cavaquinho and [Nelson Sargento](/wiki/Nelson_Sargento "Nelson Sargento").[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENuzzi2017-368)[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva201134-369) Known at the time as "regional", these musical ensemble based on classical guitar, cavaquinho and pandeiro, and occasionally some wind instrument, were revalued and became associated with the idea of a more authentic and genuine samba.[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015203-362) From then on, the idea of forming samba vocal-instrumental groups for professional presentations matured and, with the success obtained by groups such as [A Voz do Morro](/w/index.php?title=A_Voz_do_Morro&action=edit&redlink=1 "A Voz do Morro (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/A_Voz_do_Morro "pt:A Voz do Morro")] and [Os Cinco Crioulos](/w/index.php?title=Os_Cinco_Crioulos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Os Cinco Crioulos (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Os_Cinco_Crioulos "pt:Os Cinco Crioulos")], boosted the creation of other groups composed only by sambistas with direct or indirect ties with the samba schools in the following years, such as the groups [Os Originais do Samba](/wiki/Os_Originais_do_Samba "Os Originais do Samba"), Nosso Samba, Brazil Ritmo 67, Os Batuqueiros, Exporta-samba, among others.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201572–75-371) Two other significant performances from this moment of aesthetic revaluation of traditional urban samba were "Telecoteco opus N ° 1", with Cyro Monteiro and Dilermando Pinheiro, which was shown at Teatro Opinião,[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanches2004-372) and "O samba pede passagem", which brought together veterans [Ismael Silva](/wiki/Ismael_Silva_(musician) "Ismael Silva (musician)") and [Aracy de Almeida](/wiki/Aracy_de_Almeida "Aracy de Almeida") with the young artists [Baden Powell](/wiki/Baden_Powell_(guitarist) "Baden Powell (guitarist)"), [Sidney Miller](/w/index.php?title=Sidney_Miller_(composer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sidney Miller (composer) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sidney_Miller "pt:Sidney Miller")] and [MPB4](/wiki/MPB4 "MPB4"), among others.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015103-365)[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReis2018-373)

In this context of the effervescence of the samba resistance movements, the radio show "Adelzon Alves, o amigo da madrugada" ("Adelzon Alves, the friend of the dawn") has appeared.[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry2015128-374) Presented by radio broadcaster Adelzon Alves on [Rádio Globo](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Globo "Rádio Globo") in Rio de Janeiro, the radio program dedicated a repertoire exclusively dedicated to the samba[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201541–42-375) – in a scenario in which radio before the supremacy of television as a major means of communication in Brazil had become a disseminator of music recorded on disc.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry2015105–106-376) Faced with the hegemony of Anglo-American rock and Jovem Guarda, especially due to the influence of record labels on commercial broadcasters in the country,[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201541–42-375) Adelzon Alves' radio show became the main spokesman for samba and sambistas from Rio de Janeiro on the media and a major propagator of terms, which reverberate until today, referring to the legacy of the universe of "samba do morro" as national music "of resistance" and "root".[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenzecry201541–42,_111-377)[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015144-378)

In addition to the strength of Jovem Guarda, a movement catapulted by the eponymous program shown by [TV Record](/wiki/RecordTV "RecordTV"), Brazilian music at that time experienced the emergence of a new generation of post-bossa-nova artists who, reknowed in the scope of the "Brazilian song festivals" era, became the embryo of the so-called [MPB](/wiki/M%C3%BAsica_popular_brasileira "Música popular brasileira").[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDantas201612-379)[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConcagh201732-380) One of those most notable names was the composer [Chico Buarque](/wiki/Chico_Buarque "Chico Buarque"), author of sambas such as "Apesar de Você",[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015152-381) which became classics of the genre.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015151-364) Against the ideological disputes between the acoustic guitar (an instrument traditional in Brazilian music genres and synonymous with national music) and electric guitars (seen as an "Americanized" instrument in Brazilian music) that characterized these Brazilian song festivals,[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019115-288) the beginning sambista [Martinho da Vila](/wiki/Martinho_da_Vila "Martinho da Vila") entered "Menina moça", a stylized [samba de partido-alto](/wiki/Partido_alto "Partido alto"), in the third Festival of Brazilian Popular Music in 1967.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015152-381)[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimentel2007a-382)[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAntunes2012-383) Although its early eliminated in this contest, this samba projected Martinho's name on the music scene of that time,[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015104-384) whose subsequent successes paved the way for the affirmation in the music industry of this type of samba characterized by strong chorus and, normally, three solo parts.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005184-385)

As the aesthetic orientation towards young music of that time, these "song festivals" practically ignored the samba, which generated criticism from sambistas such as Elton Medeiros, who claimed the inclusion of the "truly Brazilian music" in these musical contests.[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015133-386) Against this trend, the first Bienal do Samba took place in 1968,[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015104-384)[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos201567-387)[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENapolitano2018-388) a year also characterized by the release of Paulinho da Viola's first solo album and also of another studio album by this composer in a duet with Elton Medeiros.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015152-381)[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerreira2018b-389) At the beginning of the following decade, Paulinho consolidated his prestige with the commercial success of the samba "Foi um rio que passou na minha vida" and also as a producer of the first studio album of the [Velha Guarda da Portela](/w/index.php?title=Velha_Guarda_da_Portela&action=edit&redlink=1 "Velha Guarda da Portela (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Velha_Guarda_da_Portela "pt:Velha Guarda da Portela")] samba group.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019115-288)[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015152-381)[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrança2007-390)

### Samba and the expansion of the Brazilian music industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Samba and the expansion of the Brazilian music industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Nelson_Cavaquinho_%281973%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Nelson_Cavaquinho_%281973%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nelson_Cavaquinho_(1973).tif)

Guilherme de Brito and Nelson Cavaquinho formed one of the great partnerships of samba.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Clara_Nunes_no_Zicartola.jpg/250px-Clara_Nunes_no_Zicartola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clara_Nunes_no_Zicartola.jpg)

Clara Nunes, the first Brazilian female singer to surpass the mark of 100 thousand copies sold for a single LP.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Beth_Carvalho%2C_1970.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Beth_Carvalho%2C_1970.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beth_Carvalho,_1970.tif)

Beth Carvalho, the singer who gave visibility to the "backyard pagodes" in Rio's suburbs.

Between 1968 and 1979, Brazil experienced a huge growth in the production and consumption of cultural goods.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDias199740-391)[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMachado20062–8-392) During this period, there was a strong expansion of the music industry in the country, which consolidated itself as one of the largest world markets.[[nb 14]](#cite_note-394) Among the main factors for the expansion of the Brazilian market were: the consolidation of [MPB production](/wiki/M%C3%BAsica_popular_brasileira "Música popular brasileira") stimulated by artists such as [Elis Regina](/wiki/Elis_Regina "Elis Regina"), [Chico Buarque](/wiki/Chico_Buarque "Chico Buarque"), [Caetano Veloso](/wiki/Caetano_Veloso "Caetano Veloso"), [Gilberto Gil](/wiki/Gilberto_Gil "Gilberto Gil"), [Gal Costa](/wiki/Gal_Costa "Gal Costa"), [Maria Bethania](/wiki/Maria_Beth%C3%A2nia "Maria Bethânia"),[[nb 15]](#cite_note-396) and also in the segment of sentimental songs, drawn sales champion [Roberto Carlos](/wiki/Roberto_Carlos_(singer) "Roberto Carlos (singer)");[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDias199745-397) the establishment of [LP](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") as a dominant medium format, where it was possible to insert several compositions on the same record, and also made the artist more important than his songs individually;[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDias199745–46-398) the significant participation of foreign music in the Brazilian market, with the predominance of young music on the country charts, and the growth of the international repertoire on the soap opera soundtracks, mainly on [TV Globo](/wiki/TV_Globo "TV Globo").[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDias199747–51-399)

Another important aspect in the phonographic sector of the period was technological, with a modernization of recording studios in Brazil that approached international technical standards,[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMachado20062–8-392) and the consolidation of foreign record labels in the country, such as [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") and the [WEA](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group").[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015153-400) This Brazilian entry in the scope of the global cultural industry also profoundly affected the samba universe,[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015154-401) which became one of the mass phenomena of the national music market of that decade represented by the appearance, on the list of best selling records of the period, of studio albums by artists such as [Martinho da Vila](/wiki/Martinho_da_Vila "Martinho da Vila"), Originals of Samba, [Agepê](/wiki/Agep%C3%AA "Agepê"), [Beth Carvalho](/wiki/Beth_Carvalho "Beth Carvalho"), [Clara Nunes](/wiki/Clara_Nunes "Clara Nunes"), [Alcione](/wiki/Alcione_Nazareth "Alcione Nazareth"), [Jair Rodrigues](/wiki/Jair_Rodrigues "Jair Rodrigues") and [Benito de Paula](/wiki/Benito_di_Paula "Benito di Paula"), among others, and of [sambas-enredo](/wiki/Samba-enredo "Samba-enredo") of Rio samba schools.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVicente200276-402)

In the stronghold of traditional samba, the first LPs of veteran composers [Donga](/wiki/Donga_(musician) "Donga (musician)"), [Cartola](/wiki/Cartola "Cartola") and [Nelson Cavaquinho](/wiki/Nelson_Cavaquinho "Nelson Cavaquinho") were released.[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAragão2019-403)[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerreira2020c-404) Two other composers already established in this environment, [Candeia](/wiki/Candeia "Candeia") and [Dona Ivone Lara](/wiki/Dona_Ivone_Lara "Dona Ivone Lara") also debuted with solo works in the phonographic market.[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977137-405)[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoutinho2018-406) The same happened in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") with the releases of the first [Adoniran Barbosa](/wiki/Adoniran_Barbosa "Adoniran Barbosa") and [Paulo Vanzolini](/wiki/Paulo_Vanzolini "Paulo Vanzolini") studio albums.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes197767-407)[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAragão2019-403) Revealed in the previous decade, the sambistas [Paulinho da Viola](/wiki/Paulinho_da_Viola "Paulinho da Viola") and Martinho da Vila consolidated themselves as two of the great names of success in the samba in the 1970s, which also saw the emergence of singers-songwriters [Roberto Ribeiro](/wiki/Roberto_Ribeiro "Roberto Ribeiro") and [João Nogueira](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Nogueira "João Nogueira").[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015153-400) Among the singers of the new generation, the names of Clara Nunes, Beth Carvalho and Alcione emerged as the great female samba singers in the Brazilian music industry, whose good record sales – marked by the appreciation of songs by the composers of the Rio de Janeiro samba schools – contributed greatly for the popularity of samba.[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano2015209-408)[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERechetnicou201833,_47-409) In addition to this triad of singers were also added [Leci Brandão](/wiki/Leci_Brand%C3%A3o "Leci Brandão"), who was already a member of the composer wing of [Estação Primeira de Mangueira](/wiki/Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Primeira_de_Mangueira "Estação Primeira de Mangueira"),[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa201691–92-410) and [Cristina Buarque](/wiki/Cristina_Buarque "Cristina Buarque") (sister of Chico Buarque), with a rescue effort for samba and sambistas from samba schools.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015155-411) Among the new composers, [Paulo Cesar Pinheiro](/wiki/Paulo_C%C3%A9sar_Pinheiro "Paulo César Pinheiro"), [Nei Lopes](/wiki/Nei_Lopes "Nei Lopes"), [Wilson Moreira](/wiki/Wilson_Moreira "Wilson Moreira") stood out,[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelloSeveriano2015209-408) in addition to the duo [Aldir Blanc](/wiki/Aldir_Blanc "Aldir Blanc") and [João Bosco](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Bosco "João Bosco").[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015155-411)

Under this same context of the expansion of samba in the Brazilian phonographic market of the 1970s, the music industry invested in a less traditional and more sentimental line of samba, whose simplified rhythmic structure left percussion – the main feature of samba – a little sideways.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015271-45)[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVianna2016-412) Rejected as tacky and kitsch by both the most respected musicians in the country and by critics, this formula was stigmatized under the derogatory term of "sambão-joia".[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015271-45)[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorrea_Lima2017-413)[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlice_Cruz201112-414) Despite this, this most romantic samba has become a great commercial success in the repertoire of singers such as [Luiz Ayrão](/w/index.php?title=Luiz_Ayr%C3%A3o&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luiz Ayrão (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luiz_Ayr%C3%A3o "pt:Luiz Ayrão")], [Luiz Américo](/w/index.php?title=Luiz_Am%C3%A9rico&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luiz Américo (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luiz_Am%C3%A9rico "pt:Luiz Américo")], [Gilson de Souza](/wiki/Gilson_de_Souza "Gilson de Souza"), Benito Di Paula and Agepê,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015271-45)[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlice_Cruz201112-414) as well as the duo [Antônio Carlos e Jocáfi](/w/index.php?title=Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_e_Joc%C3%A1fi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antônio Carlos e Jocáfi (page does not exist)"), authors of the world famous samba "Você abusou".[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019116-350)[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaixão2011-415)

Another bet of the phonographic industry of the time was [partido-alto](/wiki/Partido_alto "Partido alto") collective records,[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaboiaMartini2016b-416) a traditional form of samba that is often sung in the terreiros (the samba school headquarters) in Rio de Janeiro and in the usual "pagodes" – festive gatherings, with music, food and drink – since the first decades of the 20th century.[[nb 16]](#cite_note-418) With remote African roots, this sub-genre is characterized by a highly percussive [pandeiro](/wiki/Pandeiro "Pandeiro") beat (using the palm of the hand in the center of the instrument for snapping), a greater tone harmony (usually played by a set of percussion instruments normally [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo"), pandeiro and [tamborim](/wiki/Tamborim "Tamborim") and accompanied by a [cavaquinho](/wiki/Cavaquinho "Cavaquinho") and/or classical guitar)[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005-419) and the art of singing and creating improvised verses, almost always in the character of challenge or contest.[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015211–214-420) This essence based on improvisation was taken to the record studios, where partido-alto became a style with more musicality and made with more concise verses and written solos, instead of improvised and spontaneous singing according to traditional canons.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005184-385)[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbin2003251–252-421) This stylized partido-alto was released on several collective LPs, released during the 1970s, whose titles included the subgenre's own name, such as "Bambas do Partido Alto",[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMMuB2020c-422) "A Fina Flor do Partido Alto"[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMMuB2020d-423) and "Isto Que É Partido Alto",[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMMuB2020e-424) which included samba composers such as Anézio, Aniceto, Candeia, Casquinha, Joãozinho da Pecadora, Luiz Grande and Wilson Moreira, although not all were versed in the art of improvisation.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005184-385) Another artist who stood out as a *partideiro* was [Bezerra da Silva](/wiki/Bezerra_da_Silva "Bezerra da Silva"), a singer who would be noteworthy in the following decade with sambas similar to the partido-alto and themed in the world and in the underworld of Rio's favelas.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcez1999-425)[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2011104-426)

The 1970s were also a time of major changes in Rio de Janeiro samba schools, and the music industry began to invest in the annual production of LPs of the [sambas de enredo](/wiki/Samba-enredo "Samba-enredo") presented at the carnival parades.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015152-381) In the early years, it was common to release up to two albums, the first containing the sambas-enredo of the parades and the second with sambas depicting the history of each samba school.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015152-381) Beginning in 1974, the annual release began to focus on a single LP for each first and second division of Rio carnival parades[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerreira2018a-427)

Even during this period, "rodas de samba" ("samba circles") began to spread as a fever throughout Rio de Janeiro and other Brazilian cities.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019115-288)[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERocha_Lima2020-428) Originally restricted to the backyards of sambistas' residences and the samba school headquarters, these informal meetings have taken on a new meaning in clubs, theaters, steakhouses, among others, with the promotion of "rodas de samba" with stage and microphones and the participation of sambistas linked to samba schools.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERocha_Lima2020-428) Meanwhile, new "rodas de samba" were formed informally in the suburbs of Rio de Janeiro, the result of which would lead to the germ, in the late 1970s, of a new and successful sub-genre of modern samba in the 1980s.[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015243–244-429)

### Pagode, a new samba renewal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pagode, a new samba renewal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/01_jorgearagao.jpg/250px-01_jorgearagao.jpg)](/wiki/File:01_jorgearagao.jpg)

The sambista Jorge Aragão, who was part of Fundo de Quintal first line-up.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Zeca_Pagodinho.jpg/250px-Zeca_Pagodinho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zeca_Pagodinho.jpg)

The sambista Zeca Pagodinho, one of the main singers of the pagode.

Originally designated in the samba universe for the musical meetings of sambistas and, soon, also extending to the sambas sung in them,[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005177–178-430) the term pagode became popular with the resignification of the "rodas de samba" in Rio de Janeiro, from the 1970s,[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005178-431) with the "pagodes" or "pagodes de mesa" ("pagode circles"), where sambistas gathered around a large table, often located in a residential "backyard", in opposition to the fashionable samba circles made in clubs and the like.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019126-432)[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015207-433) Some of the most famous pagodes in the city were the Pagode of Clube do Samba (made at [João Nogueira](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Nogueira "João Nogueira")'s residence in [Méier](/wiki/M%C3%A9ier "Méier")), Terreirão da Tia Doca (with the rehearsals of the Portela old guard sambists in [Oswaldo Cruz](/wiki/Oswaldo_Cruz "Oswaldo Cruz")), of Pagode of Arlindinho (organized by [Arlindo Cruz](/wiki/Arlindo_Cruz "Arlindo Cruz") em [Cascadura](/wiki/Cascadura,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Cascadura, Rio de Janeiro")) and, mainly, the pagode of the carnival block [Cacique de Ramos](/wiki/Cacique_de_Ramos "Cacique de Ramos"), in the suburban area of Leopoldina.[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira2003138-434)[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015207–208-435)[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza1983-436)

In the 1980s, pagodes became a fever throughout Rio de Janeiro.[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaballero1985-437)[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira200397-438)[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005179–180-439) And, far beyond simple places of entertainment, they became radiating centers of a new musical language that expressed itself with a new interpretive and totally renewed style of samba that was embedded in the tradition of the [partido-alto](/wiki/Partido_alto "Partido alto").[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015209-440)[[425]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019126–127-441) Among the innovations of this new samba and marked by refinement in melodies and innovations in harmony and percussion with the accompaniment of instruments such as [tan-tan](/wiki/Tan-tan "Tan-tan") (in place of the [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo")), the [hand-repique](/wiki/Hand-repique "Hand-repique") and the [four-string banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo") with [cavaquinho](/wiki/Cavaquinho "Cavaquinho") tuning.[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza1983-436)[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019127-442)[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza1985-443)[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015208-444)

The debut of this kind of samba in the recording studios occurred in 1980 with [Fundo de Quintal](/wiki/Fundo_de_Quintal "Fundo de Quintal"),[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019126-432)[[429]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrottaSilva201510–11-445) musical group sponsored by [Beth Carvalho](/wiki/Beth_Carvalho "Beth Carvalho").[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza1983-436)[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAraujo2019-446)[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaltar2019-447) In its first works, Fundo de Quintal gave visibility not only to this new samba, but also to composers such as [Almir Guineto](/wiki/Almir_Guineto "Almir Guineto"), [Arlindo Cruz](/wiki/Arlindo_Cruz "Arlindo Cruz"), [Jorge Aragão](/wiki/Jorge_Arag%C3%A3o "Jorge Aragão") – all members of the group – and [Luiz Carlos da Vila](/w/index.php?title=Luiz_Carlos_da_Vila&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luiz Carlos da Vila (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luiz_Carlos_da_Vila "pt:Luiz Carlos da Vila")] – this one linked to the Cacique de Ramos pagodes.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019126-432)[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015208-444) On this way opened by Fundo de Quintal, in 1985 the collective studio album called "Raça Brasileira" was released,[[432]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMMuB2020f-448) which revealed to the general public singers such as [Jovelina Pérola Negra](/wiki/Jovelina_P%C3%A9rola_Negra "Jovelina Pérola Negra") and [Zeca Pagodinho](/wiki/Zeca_Pagodinho "Zeca Pagodinho").[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouzaMariaCezimbraAragão1986-449) Especially prioritizing [partido-alto](/wiki/Partido_alto "Partido alto") sambas, this LP, as well as the works since 1979 by Beth Carvalho, Almir Guineto and the group Fundo de Quintal, formed the new sub-genre that ended up being called [pagode](/wiki/Pagode "Pagode") by the Brazilian music industry.[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015208-444)[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2005180-450)[[435]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019125-451)

The novelty of the pagode in the Brazilian music scene occurred at a time of major reorganization of the music industry in the country, whose investments in the first half of the 1980s had been concentrated mainly on [Brazilian rock](/wiki/Brazilian_rock "Brazilian rock") and [children's music](/wiki/Children%27s_music "Children's music").[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019126-432)[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015156-452) Although some samba artists had some commercial success in the period, such as [Bezerra da Silva](/wiki/Bezerra_da_Silva "Bezerra da Silva"), Almir Guineto[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVicente2002238–239-453) and [Agepê](/wiki/Agep%C3%AA "Agepê") – who, in 1984, became the first samba singer to surpass the mark of 1 million copies sold on a single LP[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorrea_Lima2017-413) -, the moment was not promising for samba in the commercial scope. Very popular performers like [Beth Carvalho](/wiki/Beth_Carvalho "Beth Carvalho"), [Clara Nunes](/wiki/Clara_Nunes "Clara Nunes"), [João Nogueira](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Nogueira "João Nogueira") and [Roberto Ribeiro](/wiki/Roberto_Ribeiro "Roberto Ribeiro") pulled the drop in sales of records of the genre.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019116-350) Disgusted by the little recognition and interest in promoting his work, [Paulinho da Viola](/wiki/Paulinho_da_Viola "Paulinho da Viola") left the [Warner Music](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") label in 1984 and only returned to having an album released at the end of that decade.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoda_Viva1989-454)

With the success of the LP "Raça Brasileira", the pagode phenomenon experienced a period of commercial growth in the Brazilian phonographic market.[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouzaMariaCezimbraAragão1986-449) The main artists in this sub-genre reached the top of the success charts and became known nationally thanks to exposure in the mainstream media and the growing investments of record labels stimulated by huge sales since 1986, pulled by both the LPs of the already established Almir Guineto and Fundo de Quintal – the great paradigm of the subgenre – and for the debut works of Zeca Pagodinho, Marquinhos Satã and Jovelina Pérola Negra.[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouzaMariaCezimbraAragão1986-449)[[439]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza2003247-455) Although there was a certain cooling of the interest of record labels and the media even during the second half of the 1980s, pagode established itself as an important subgenre of samba.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopes2019126-432)[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015208-444)

In the 1990s, a new generation of artists emerged who shared, to some extent, similar characteristics, such as the incorporation of musical elements traditionally uncommon in the traditional samba, and a repertoire devoted largely to romantic lyrics.[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPinto201386–87-456) Initially seen by the phonographic industry and by the media as a continuation of the pagode of the previous decade,[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015142-457) this new wave was later characterized under the label of "pagode romântico" ("romantic pagode") – or also "pagode paulista", due to the large number of artists of this scene that emerged mainly from [São Paulo state](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), although there were also names from [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") and [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") states.[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015157-458)[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBorges2019-459)

This distinction was established precisely because the samba made by these new artists and musical groups – although it maintained some similarities with the standard enshrined in the Fundo de Quintal[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015142-457) – did not have the samba musicians of the previous decade as a major musical reference nor did it keep traditional and informal aspects of matrixes of urban samba.[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira2003165-460)[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotta2006158–159-461) For example, the studio recordings of a large part of these samba bands, such as [Raça Negra](/wiki/Ra%C3%A7a_Negra "Raça Negra"), gave up the use of instruments common to the 1980s pagode – such as hand-repique, tan-tan and banjo – in exchange for instrumentation characteristic of international pop music from that period, especially the saxophone and the [electronic keyboard](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard").[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotta2006153-462) The use of these pop music instruments was less or more common to each group,[[nb 17]](#cite_note-464) but their purpose was the same, that is, the use of [samplers](/wiki/Sampler_(musical_instrument) "Sampler (musical instrument)") and keyboards to reproduce the sound of various instruments.[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015157-458) Despite these dilutions, the "romantic pagode" achieved great commercial success in the Brazilian phonographic market and in the mass media, highlighting samba groups such as [Art Popular](/w/index.php?title=Art_Popular&action=edit&redlink=1 "Art Popular (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Art_Popular "pt:Art Popular")], [Negritude Júnior](/wiki/Negritude_J%C3%BAnior "Negritude Júnior"), [Exaltasamba](/wiki/Exaltasamba "Exaltasamba"), [Katinguelê](/wiki/Katinguel%C3%AA "Katinguelê"), Raça Negra, [Só Pra Contrariar](/wiki/S%C3%B3_Pra_Contrariar "Só Pra Contrariar"), [Soweto](/w/index.php?title=Soweto_(band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soweto (band) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soweto_(banda) "pt:Soweto (banda)")], among others.[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015208-444)[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouza200318-465)

### Samba in the 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Samba in the 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/26_Pr%C3%AAmio_da_M%C3%BAsica_Brasileira_%2818525382718%29.jpg/250px-26_Pr%C3%AAmio_da_M%C3%BAsica_Brasileira_%2818525382718%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:26_Pr%C3%AAmio_da_M%C3%BAsica_Brasileira_(18525382718).jpg)

[Xande de Pilares](/wiki/Xande_de_Pilares "Xande de Pilares"), a sambista who emerged as the [Grupo Revelação](/wiki/Grupo_Revela%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Grupo Revelação") singer-songwriter and leader.

During the second half of the 1990s, the increase in the illegal sale of [cassette tapes](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape") and, mainly, compact discs caused a deep crisis in the music industry in Brazil,[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERangel_dos_Santos201020-466)[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFloriano_do_Nascimento200580-467) which worsened, from the 2000s, with the possibility of digital download, often free of charge, of musical works via the internet.[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFloriano_do_Nascimento2005159–160-468) In this context, there was a sharp drop in the commercialization of official samba records and their sub-genres, especially [pagode](/wiki/Pagode "Pagode").[[452]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerez2000-469) Samba groups of huge commercial success in the 1990s, such as [Raça Negra](/wiki/Ra%C3%A7a_Negra "Raça Negra") and [Só Pra Contrariar](/wiki/S%C3%B3_Pra_Contrariar "Só Pra Contrariar"), saw their sales drop substantially at the turn of the 21st century.[[452]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerez2000-469)[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira2001-470) In addition, in a space of a few decades, samba songs played in the media have declined, with the genre it is almost always represented by the sub-genre pagode in the Brazilian charts.[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaltarBryan2016-471) Of the 100 most heard artists on Brazilian radio between 2010 and 2019 on the [Crowley Official Broadcast Chart](/wiki/Crowley_Broadcast_Analysis "Crowley Broadcast Analysis"), only 11 were from samba – and all from pagode.[[455]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEXavier2020-472) In another survey, carried out jointly between [Kantar Ibope Media](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Public_Opinion_and_Statistics "Brazilian Institute of Public Opinion and Statistics") and Crowley Broadcast Analysis, the pagode corresponded to only 9% of the radio audience in Brazil in 2019, too far from the dominant [sertanejo](/wiki/Sertanejo_music "Sertanejo music") (Brazilian country music genre), whose slice represented about one third of the radio audience in the country.[[456]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrêda2020-473)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Funchal%2C_Cortejo_Aleg%C3%B3rico_de_Carnaval_%282026%29_07.jpg/250px-Funchal%2C_Cortejo_Aleg%C3%B3rico_de_Carnaval_%282026%29_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Funchal,_Cortejo_Aleg%C3%B3rico_de_Carnaval_(2026)_07.jpg)

Samba female dancers

Even so, the first two decades of the 21st century confirmed the pagode as the hegemonic reference of samba in the Brazilian music industry.[[457]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrottaSilva20152-474) In the first decade of this century, new artists emerged commercially, such as the samba bands [Grupo Revelação](/wiki/Grupo_Revela%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Grupo Revelação"), [Sorriso Maroto](/wiki/Sorriso_Maroto "Sorriso Maroto") and [Turma do Pagode](/w/index.php?title=Turma_do_Pagode&action=edit&redlink=1 "Turma do Pagode (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turma_do_Pagode "pt:Turma do Pagode")], and some singers who left their original samba groups to launch a solo career, such as [Péricles](/wiki/P%C3%A9ricles_(singer) "Péricles (singer)") (former [Exaltasamba](/wiki/Exaltasamba "Exaltasamba")), [Belo](/w/index.php?title=Belo_(singer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belo (singer) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Belo_(cantor) "pt:Belo (cantor)")] (former Soweto) and [Alexandre Pires](/wiki/Alexandre_Pires "Alexandre Pires") (formerly of Só Pra Contrariar). In the following decade, it was the turn of [Xande de Pilares](/wiki/Xande_de_Pilares "Xande de Pilares") and [Thiaguinho](/wiki/Thiaguinho_(singer) "Thiaguinho (singer)"), former vocalists of Revelação and Exaltasamba respectively, and of singers [Mumuzinho](/wiki/Mumuzinho "Mumuzinho"), [Ferrugem](/wiki/Ferrugem_(singer) "Ferrugem (singer)") and [Dilsinho](/wiki/Dilsinho "Dilsinho").[[458]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrêda2019a-475)[[459]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENobile2013-476) A characteristic common to all these artists was the significant amount of live album releases instead of traditional studio albums.[[460]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrêda2019b-477)[[461]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrêdaMariani2019-478) This gained even more strength with the development of [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"), a platform for [digital music](/wiki/Digital_audio "Digital audio") that became popular in the 2010s.[[462]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDias2020-479)

Outside the hegemonic commercial scope of the subgenre pagode, the late 1990s was also a period of great visibility and notoriety for the most traditional samba in Rio de Janeiro.[[463]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPontes20118-480) A new generation of musicians emerged in "rodas de samba" that spread through several neighborhoods in the city, especially in [Lapa](/wiki/Lapa,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Lapa, Rio de Janeiro"), the central region of the city that started to concentrate several bars and restaurants with live music.[[464]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimentel2011-481) For having identified with the bohemian neighborhood, this movement became known informally as "samba da Lapa".[[465]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015169–170-482) With a repertoire composed of classics sambas and without concessions to more modern sub-genres,[[465]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015169–170-482) this new circuit promoted the meeting between beginning and veteran musicians from several generations of sambistas, all identified with the traditional elements that make up the urban Carioca samba.[[463]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPontes20118-480) Among some artists who acted in the scope of samba circles in this neighborhood, were [Teresa Cristina](/wiki/Teresa_Cristina_(singer) "Teresa Cristina (singer)") and Semente group, [Nilze Carvalho](/w/index.php?title=Nilze_Carvalho&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nilze Carvalho (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nilze_Carvalho "pt:Nilze Carvalho")] and Sururu na Roda group, Luciane Menezes and Dobrando a Esquina group, Eduardo Gallotti and Anjos da Lua group, among others, besides veterans such as [Áurea Martins](/wiki/%C3%81urea_Martins "Áurea Martins").[[465]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015169–170-482)[[466]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimentel2007b-483) And later, Edu Krieger and Moyseis Marques has appeared.[[464]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimentel2011-481)[[467]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimentel2010-484) Other new artists linked to the samba traditions, but without direct ties to the Lapa carioca movement, emerged such as [Dudu Nobre](/wiki/Dudu_Nobre "Dudu Nobre")[[468]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrotta2006227–232-485) and [Diogo Nogueira](/wiki/Diogo_Nogueira "Diogo Nogueira"),[[469]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPontes201197-486) in addition to [Fabiana Cozza](/wiki/Fabiana_Cozza "Fabiana Cozza") in São Paulo.[[470]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimentel2006-487)

In the institutional field, the Brazilian [National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Historic_and_Artistic_Heritage "National Institute of Historic and Artistic Heritage") declared in 2007 the modern Carioca samba and its matrixes samba de terreiro, partido-alto and samba-enredo as Intangible Cultural Heritage in Brazil.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIphan201423-53)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUchôa2007-55)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFigueiredo2007-56)

## Urban samba instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samba&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Urban samba instruments")]

With basically **2**  
**2** rhythm and varied tempo, the urban samba is played by percussion instruments[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas2015220-47)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBolão200922–44-48)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos2018107–109-49) and accompanied by string instruments. In certain areas, other wind instruments were added.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETinhorão1990296–297-50)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2006153–154-51)
